title: Conversational Journey Maps and Price Sensitivity Tests for Technical Buyers
preview:
Overview

This page is a consolidated sales enablement handbook for GTM teams working with technical buyers evaluating Redwood Inference for conversational products. It couples user journey maps (developer and product-owner perspectives) with empirical price-sensitivity test templates and an evidence-backed competitive brief. The goal is to equip Sales Engineers, Solutions Architects, and AEs with repeatable conversation scripts, testable pricing scenarios, and clear security FAQ answers for prospect diligence.

Use cases in scope
- Real-time chatbots (sub-200ms token latency targets)
- Multi-turn assistants with long context (up to 64k tokens)
- High-throughput embedding and reranking pipelines
- Edge / hybrid deployments for regulated customers

Quick reference: what to carry to a technical call
1) Two-slide technical narrative (latency/cost tradeoffs + fallback story)
2) One-page pricing sensitivity table with 3 scenarios (pilot / scale / enterprise commit)
3) Live demo checklist (API keys, sample prompts, sample dataset, model variant to demo)
4) Security evidence bundle pointer (redwood-kms-evidence.pdf)

Section 1 — Conversational journey maps
Purpose: map buyer personas to technical triggers, evaluation criteria, and Redwood talking points. For each persona, we present a short narrative, key metrics they care about, and suggested demo prompts.

Persona A: Platform Engineer (internal platform team)
- Evaluation triggers: standardize inference across teams, predictable unit economics, RBAC and compliance integration.
- Key metrics: tokens/sec per GPU, p95 latency, per-token cost, failure modes.
- Talking points: Dedicated pools, autoscale policies, per-route token accounting in Redwood Console, KMS integrations.
- Demo prompts: multi-tenant routing example + trace showing per-route latency breakdown.

Persona B: Product Lead (customer-facing feature owner)
- Evaluation triggers: response quality, consistent latency, rollback capability during quality regressions.
- Key metrics: quality eval pass rate on golden prompts, 99th percentile total time-to-first-token, canary rollout success rate.
- Talking points: Redwood Optimize for recommended quantization & batching, automatic rollback policies, prompt-set linked quality alerts.
- Demo prompts: A/B response comparison to show model variant choice impact and Redwood’s rollout controls.

Persona C: Security & Compliance Officer
- Evaluation triggers: data residency, auditability, key controls.
- Key metrics: encryption at rest/in transit, audit log retention, isolation topology (VPC + Private).
- Talking points: Private deployments (VPC/on-prem), HSM/KMS integrations, per-request audit IDs, SOC2 and ISO evidence pointer.
- Demo prompts: audit log drilldown + show request header obfuscation and PII scrubbing options.

Section 2 — Price sensitivity test templates (method + scenarios)

Overview: We recommend running three price-sensitivity experiments during a pilot to calibrate required capacity and convert to a committed plan. The templates below are designed for customer pilots that will later become Dedicated or Private deployments.

Experiment setup checklist:
- Duration: 7–14 days per experiment
- Dataset: 5k representative user interactions (include extremes: long context, short prompts, multimodal if applicable)
- Metrics to capture: avg tokens/request, p50/p95 latency, cost per 1k tokens (hosted), end-to-end throughput
- Instrumentation: enable token-level billing metadata, route-level latency traces, and KV cache hit rate in Redwood Console

Pricing scenario matrix (table)
| Scenario | Description | Key thresholds | Recommended Redwood ask | Success criteria |
|---|---:|---|---|---|
| Trial-Pilot | Low-volume proof (<= 5M tokens/month) | p95 < 500ms, cost < $0.02/1k tokens (hosted) | Free pilot credits + 2-week Dedicated trial | Evidence of consistent p95 and expected unit cost |
| Growth | Steady product usage (5M - 50M tokens/month) | p95 < 250ms, cost < $0.01/1k tokens | 6–12 month reserved capacity (Dedicated) with autoscale buffer | Unit economics within 15% of pilot projection |
| Enterprise-Commit | High volume + compliance (50M+ tokens/month or Private) | p95 < 150ms, contractual SLA 99.9% | Annual Reserved + Private deployment option and premium SLAs | Contract value aligns with expected TCO and cost-savings vs self-hosting |

Notes on negotiation levers
- Batching and caching profiles: quantify expected token reduction due to prefix caching (report cache hit rate).
- Model variants: provide cost deltas for top-performing 2–3 open models and a Redwood-optimized quantized variant.
- Commitment term: show 12 vs 36 month unit price curves and step-down discounts.

Section 3 — Proof points and compact case studies

Case study: FastReply (mid-market, conversational support)
- Problem: inconsistent SLA with prior LLM vendor, high per-session cost during peak spikes.
- What we did: migrated to Redwood Dedicated, enabled prefix caching for common support threads, and used Redwood Optimize recommendations to move to a 4-bit quantized variant for non-latency-critical routes.
- Result: 48% reduction in per-session cost, p95 latency improved from 820ms to 190ms, and successful canary rollout across 12k daily sessions.
- Materials: sample benchmarking report (redwood-fastreply-bench-2028.pdf).

Case study: FinAssist (regulated financial assistant)
- Problem: requirement for audit logs, KMS integration, and on-premise model hosting.
- What we did: deployed Redwood Private in customer VPC, integrated customer KMS with HSM-backed keys, and configured per-request audit IDs sent to the customer SIEM.
- Result: passed internal security review, met data residency requirements, and enabled product launch in an EU market.

Section 4 — Competitive brief (concise)

This section summarizes how to position Redwood against the three common counteroffers during technical evaluation. Keep this on a single page for deal rooms.

Competitor A: Large cloud provider managed endpoints
- Strengths: deep cloud integrations, global regions, brand trust.
- Redwood counter: lower unit cost for inference at scale via runtime batching + prefix/KV cache and transparent model-performance profiles; unified API across hosted/dedicated/private reduces migration friction.

Competitor B: LLM API pure-play (simple dev UX)
- Strengths: easy onboarding, strong docs, low initial friction.
- Redwood counter: comparable developer UX plus explicit production controls (SLOs, per-route cost tracking, rollout policies) and deployed options for regulated workloads.

Competitor C: Self-hosted inference frameworks
- Strengths: full control of stack and cost if large engineering org.
- Redwood counter: fewer ops overheads, predictable SLAs, Smart Routing to preserve uptime during capacity constraints, and cost-advantage analyses showing faster time-to-value.

Section 5 — Sales engineer conversation scripts and objection handling

Intro: below are short scripts to use live in a call. Keep them tight and metric-backed.

Script: latency concern
- Buyer: "We need <200ms p95 for chat."
- SE: "Understood. For comparable deployments we've delivered p95 <= 180ms using Dedicated pools with optimized kernels and continuous batching. On the pilot we instrument trace data and share a p95 baseline within 72 hours so you can validate. Would you prefer we size the Dedicated trial for peak concurrency or average load?"

Script: pricing pushback ("Your hosted price is higher than competitor")
- SE: "We often see higher apparent hosted cost if comparing raw per-token numbers. Two key differences: (1) our reported per-token cost accounts for throughput-optimizing features like KV cache and batch scheduling which reduce effective downstream cost; (2) we include guaranteed performance and rollback tooling that lower product risk. For fairness, let us run one of our sensitivity tests against your dataset and return a visible cost projection in <7 business days."

Section 6 — Security FAQ (concise answers for prospect diligence)

Q: Can customer keys be used for encryption at rest?
A: Yes. Redwood supports customer-managed keys via KMS (AWS/GCP/Azure) and HSM-backed key rotation. We provide a template integration runbook for Private deployments.

Q: How are audit logs provided?
A: Request-level audit logs with request IDs, model variant, and route metadata can be streamed to customer SIEM or stored in our encrypted audit store. Retention policies are configurable per customer.

Q: Is data used to train models?
A: No. For enterprise and Dedicated customers, data is not used to train public models. We maintain strict data-use contracts and provide an evidence package in the security bundle.

Q: What certifications do you hold?
A: Redwood maintains SOC 2 Type II and ISO 27001. Private/Enterprise customers can request our compliance package via the Contracting team.

Section 7 — Live-demo checklist and commands

Pre-call (15 minutes)
- Verify API key scoped to demo org and route-level rate limits
- Upload small sample dataset (<= 200 prompts) for on-the-fly tests
- Configure a canary route and rollback policy in Redwood Console

Minimal curl to run a sample chat (replace API_KEY and MODEL_VAR):

curl -X POST https://api.redwood.ai/v1/generate 
  -H "Authorization: Bearer API_KEY" 
  -H "Content-Type: application/json" 
  -d '{"model":"MODEL_VAR","prompt":"Summarize recent user messages","max_tokens":128}'

Post-demo deliverables
- Baseline telemetry report (p50/p95 latency, token counts, cache hit rate)
- Cost projection workbook (three scenarios from Section 2)
- Security evidence package link and next-step recommended architecture

Appendix: conversion checklist for pilots to committed deals
1) Capture baseline telemetry for pilot across peak windows
2) Validate quality regressions with Redwood Optimize prompt set testing
3) Agree on a clear success metric (SLA or cost-savings target)
4) Propose a 12-month reserved capacity term with a 10% holdback for first-month refunds if SLO missed

If you have questions or need a bespoke slide pack for an executive briefing, contact: go-to-market@redwood.ai or add a request to the 'SE resources' channel in Slack.

title: Channel Partner Win Playbook: Technical Value, Pricing & Security Matrix
preview:
Summary:
This playbook is intended for channel and strategic partners (ISVs, systems integrators, and resellers) who are evaluating or co-selling Redwood Inference with their customers. It provides crisp positioning, a compact competitive comparison, a technical integration checklist, partner-friendly pricing scenarios for pilots and proofs-of-concept (PoCs), and a security & compliance FAQ tailored to partner conversations.

Use cases targeted:
- Embedded chat and summarization inside SaaS products (low-latency interactive path)
- High-throughput batch embeddings for search and recommendation systems
- Regulated verticals (finance, healthcare) using Private/VPC deployments

Top-level positioning (elevator + extended):
- Elevator (30s): Redwood Inference delivers predictable, low-latency LLM serving across hosted, dedicated, and private deployments so your product team can embed AI features without trading off cost or reliability.
- Extended (90s): Unlike single-mode LLM vendors or raw infra providers, Redwood unifies runtime optimizations (KV-caching, continuous batching, quantization profiles) with operational controls (canary rollouts, cost breakdowns, per-route SLOs). That means faster time-to-production, lower per-token cost, and clear fallbacks during capacity events—critical for customers who need predictable user experience and controllable economics.

Key messaging pillars (pick 2–3 for each buyer type):
- Platform/CTO: Predictable performance and unit economics — dedicated pools, SLAs, and routed fallbacks reduce incident risk and TCO variance.
- Product/PM: Fast experimentation paths with the same API across hosted/dedicated/private deployments and built-in quality monitoring tied to prompt sets.
- Security/Compliance: Private deployment options (VPC or on-prem), customer-managed KMS, audit logs and RBAC compatibility for enterprise controls.
- Developer/Platform Engineer: SDKs and observability hooks (traces, per-token cost) plus recommended batching/caching profiles for workload-specific wins.

Competitive brief (short):
- Competitor: Managed LLM API X
  - Where Redwood wins: Lower latency variance for interactive chat (smart routing + KV caching), predictable dedicated capacity, and partner-friendly private deployments.
  - Where X wins: Larger model catalog in hosted-only mode; faster single-tenant onboarding for certain legacy clouds.
- Competitor: Self-hosted inference stack Y
  - Where Redwood wins: Integrated operational tooling, rollouts, transparent per-token economics, and reduced engineering overhead for maintenance and scaling.
  - Where Y wins: Absolute control over hardware and customization of kernels for niche models.
- Competitor: Cloud ML endpoints Z
  - Where Redwood wins: Consistent API surface across deployment modes and better controls for model quality regressions and rollback.
  - Where Z wins: Deep cloud integrations (e.g., native cloud billing and certain managed storage options).

Competitive comparison matrix (high level):
Product                | Hosting modes        | Predictable latency | Private deploys | Cost controls | Partner support
-----------------------|----------------------|---------------------|------------------|---------------|----------------
Redwood Inference      | Hosted, Dedicated, Private | High (SLO-backed)    | Yes (VPC/on-prem) | Batch/caching/quantization guidance | Co-sell & integrations
Managed API X          | Hosted only          | Medium              | No               | Limited       | Marketplace partners
Self-hosted Y          | Private only         | Variable            | Yes              | Engineer-managed | Services-led
Cloud Endpoint Z       | Hosted + cloud-native | Medium-high         | Restricted       | Cloud-native   | Cloud GTM

Partner GTM playbook (step-by-step):
1) Qualify and map:
   - Identify partner motion: embed (ISV), integration (SI), reseller (channel).
   - Target customer profile: attachment point (product team, platform team, security).
   - Minimum technical signal: expected QPS, max TTFB target, latency SLO (p50/p95), model size.
2) Pilot design (7–21 days recommended):
   - Start with hosted sandbox keys and up to 100k tokens free. Validate latency and output quality.
   - Parallel dedicated pilot: provision a 1–2 GPU dedicated pool for throughput-sensitive customers.
   - Define success metrics: p95 latency <= target, cost per 1k tokens <= budget, RTT stability, and qualitative output accuracy on 10–20 representative prompts.
3) Security/contract checks:
   - Determine data residency needs, KMS/HSM requirements, and whether Private deployment is needed.
   - Provide partner with SOC2/ISO evidence and support level options.
4) Commercial & packaging:
   - Offer a 60–90 day pilot credit, with option to convert to committed dedicated capacity or Private license.
   - Co-sell compensation: standard margin schedule or referral fee; for strategic deals consider co-investment in POCs.
5) Scale & embed:
   - Migrate from sandbox to dedicated or Private.
   - Implement canary rollouts for model updates and set up Redwood Optimize recommendations for quantization and batching.

Pricing guidance for partner pilots (example scenarios):
- Embedded exploratory pilot (ISV): 60 days hosted sandbox -> free credits up to $10k spend. Conversion: example pricing for hosted tier = $0.0025 per 1k tokens for generation + $0.0006 per embedding.
- Throughput pilot (integration partner): Short dedicated pilot with one reserved A100-equivalent GPU pool for 30 days. Pilot rate = discounted reserved rate (e.g., 30% off list) with conversion to monthly commit.
- Regulated customer (Private): Fixed license + deployment support: one-time platform fee (example: $75k) + annual maintenance (10–15% of license) + per-GPU usage for inference.
Note: These numbers are illustrative. Always coordinate final pricing with Redwood deal desk and finance.

Technical integration checklist (minimum viable integration):
- API & auth: Confirm API key flow, rate limits, regional endpoints. Example curl test:
  curl -X POST https://api.redwood.ai/v1/generate -H "Authorization: Bearer $REDWOOD_KEY" -d '{"model":"rwd-3b", "input":"hello"}'
- Latency profiling: Capture p50/p95 for representative prompts; capture cold-start and warm-cache cases.
- Batching/caching: Implement prefix caching for repeated context; for embedding workloads use batch sizes 32–128 depending on GPU memory.
- Tracing & observability: Wire traces (OpenTelemetry recommended) and export per-request token cost and latency metrics to partner dashboards.
- Canary & rollback: Configure a canary with 1–5% traffic, automated quality checks against prompt set, and instant fallback policy to previous model variant.
- Compliance: Validate audit logs ingestion, KMS key exchange process, and retention policy alignment.

Security & Compliance FAQ (for partner-led prospect conversations):
Q: Can a customer run Redwood privately inside their VPC or on-prem?
A: Yes. Redwood Private supports VPC deployments and air-gapped on-prem packages. Deployment patterns vary by customer size and regulatory requirements — provide reference architecture and sizing guidance.

Q: How is encryption handled?
A: Customer data in transit is TLS 1.2+; at-rest encryption uses customer-managed KMS keys where requested for Private deployments. We support integration with AWS KMS, GCP KMS, and HSMs for key lifecycle needs.

Q: What audit evidence do you provide?
A: SOC 2 Type II attestation is available for hosted offerings. For Private deployments we provide deployment checklists and can support third-party attestation needs via professional services.

Q: How do you prevent model regressions from reaching production?
A: Redwood Optimize ties quality monitors to prompt-based evals. Use canary rollouts with automated checks (e.g., BLEU, ROUGE, classification accuracy) and automatic rollback rules.

Q: Where does customer data go when using Hosted?
A: Data is processed in the region selected for the tenant and is not used for model training. Logs and telemetry retention is configurable; sensitive content redaction patterns can be applied at the request of the customer.

Q: What isolation guarantees exist for Dedicated deployments?
A: Dedicated capacity runs in logically isolated GPU pools with tenant-level quotas, dedicated networking, and optional private networking endpoints.

Ready-to-use sales assets and playbook attachments:
- One-pager: Partner technical sell sheet (link: internal asset ID: PA-2029-012)
- Demo kit: Prebuilt sandbox with synthetic dataset for latency and cost benchmarking (asset ID: DK-2029-004)
- Customer references: Case study extract for SaaS search embed (CS-2028-009)

Ownership and escalation:
- Primary owner: Partnerships (Ava Chen)
- Technical owner: Platform Solutions (Diego Martinez)
- Security reviewer: Security & Compliance (Priya Nair)
- Escalation path: For pricing escalations contact Deal Desk (deal-desk@redwood.ai). For technical escalations escalate to Platform SRE oncall and CC the partner success manager.

Appendix: sample objection handling lines (short):
- "We already have a cloud endpoint": Redwood reduces operational burden and offers predictable SLOs plus a consistent API across deployment modes — you get the benefits of both managed endpoints and private control.
- "We need lower cost": Start with Redwood Optimize recommendations (quantization + batching) — partners typically see 30–60% per-token cost reductions depending on workload.

Last updated notes:
- 2029-01-10: Added pricing scenario for regulated Private deployments and updated pilot guidance.
- 2028-11-20: Initial draft and competitive matrix added.

Contact for feedback: partnerships@redwood.ai

Redwood Inference      | Hosted, Dedicated, Private | High (SLO-backed)    | Yes (VPC/on-prem) | Batch/caching/quantization guidance | Co-sell & integrations
Managed API X          | Hosted only          | Medium              | No               | Limited       | Marketplace partners
Self-hosted Y          | Private only         | Variable            | Yes              | Engineer-managed | Services-led
Cloud Endpoint Z       | Hosted + cloud-native | Medium-high         | Restricted       | Cloud-native   | Cloud GTM

Partner GTM playbook (step-by-step):
1) Qualify and map:
   - Identify partner motion: embed (ISV), integration (SI), reseller (channel).
   - Target customer profile: attachment point (product team, platform team, security).
   - Minimum technical signal: expected QPS, max TTFB target, latency SLO (p50/p95), model size.
2) Pilot design (7–21 days recommended):
   - Start with hosted sandbox keys and up to 100k tokens free. Validate latency and output quality.
   - Parallel dedicated pilot: provision a 1–2 GPU dedicated pool for throughput-sensitive customers.
   - Define success metrics: p95 latency <= target, cost per 1k tokens <= budget, RTT stability, and qualitative output accuracy on 10–20 representative prompts.
3) Security/contract checks:
   - Determine data residency needs, KMS/HSM requirements, and whether Private deployment is needed.
   - Provide partner with SOC2/ISO evidence and support level options.
4) Commercial & packaging:
   - Offer a 60–90 day pilot credit, with option to convert to committed dedicated capacity or Private license.
   - Co-sell compensation: standard margin schedule or referral fee; for strategic deals consider co-investment in POCs.
5) Scale & embed:
   - Migrate from sandbox to dedicated or Private.
   - Implement canary rollouts for model updates and set up Redwood Optimize recommendations for quantization and batching.

Pricing guidance for partner pilots (example scenarios):
- Embedded exploratory pilot (ISV): 60 days hosted sandbox -> free credits up to $10k spend. Conversion: example pricing for hosted tier = $0.0025 per 1k tokens for generation + $0.0006 per embedding.
- Throughput pilot (integration partner): Short dedicated pilot with one reserved A100-equivalent GPU pool for 30 days. Pilot rate = discounted reserved rate (e.g., 30% off list) with conversion to monthly commit.
- Regulated customer (Private): Fixed license + deployment support: one-time platform fee (example: $75k) + annual maintenance (10–15% of license) + per-GPU usage for inference.
Note: These numbers are illustrative. Always coordinate final pricing with Redwood deal desk and finance.

Technical integration checklist (minimum viable integration):
- API & auth: Confirm API key flow, rate limits, regional endpoints. Example curl test:
  curl -X POST https://api.redwood.ai/v1/generate -H "Authorization: Bearer $REDWOOD_KEY" -d '{"model":"rwd-3b", "input":"hello"}'
- Latency profiling: Capture p50/p95 for representative prompts; capture cold-start and warm-cache cases.
- Batching/caching: Implement prefix caching for repeated context; for embedding workloads use batch sizes 32–128 depending on GPU memory.
- Tracing & observability: Wire traces (OpenTelemetry recommended) and export per-request token cost and latency metrics to partner dashboards.
- Canary & rollback: Configure a canary with 1–5% traffic, automated quality checks against prompt set, and instant fallback policy to previous model variant.
- Compliance: Validate audit logs ingestion, KMS key exchange process, and retention policy alignment.

Security & Compliance FAQ (for partner-led prospect conversations):
Q: Can a customer run Redwood privately inside their VPC or on-prem?
A: Yes. Redwood Private supports VPC deployments and air-gapped on-prem packages. Deployment patterns vary by customer size and regulatory requirements — provide reference architecture and sizing guidance.

Q: How is encryption handled?
A: Customer data in transit is TLS 1.2+; at-rest encryption uses customer-managed KMS keys where requested for Private deployments. We support integration with AWS KMS, GCP KMS, and HSMs for key lifecycle needs.

Q: What audit evidence do you provide?
A: SOC 2 Type II attestation is available for hosted offerings. For Private deployments we provide deployment checklists and can support third-party attestation needs via professional services.

Q: How do you prevent model regressions from reaching production?
A: Redwood Optimize ties quality monitors to prompt-based evals. Use canary rollouts with automated checks (e.g., BLEU, ROUGE, classification accuracy) and automatic rollback rules.

Q: Where does customer data go when using Hosted?
A: Data is processed in the region selected for the tenant and is not used for model training. Logs and telemetry retention is configurable; sensitive content redaction patterns can be applied at the request of the customer.

Q: What isolation guarantees exist for Dedicated deployments?
A: Dedicated capacity runs in logically isolated GPU pools with tenant-level quotas, dedicated networking, and optional private networking endpoints.

Ready-to-use sales assets and playbook attachments:
- One-pager: Partner technical sell sheet (link: internal asset ID: PA-2029-012)
- Demo kit: Prebuilt sandbox with synthetic dataset for latency and cost benchmarking (asset ID: DK-2029-004)
- Customer references: Case study extract for SaaS search embed (CS-2028-009)

Ownership and escalation:
- Primary owner: Partnerships (Ava Chen)
- Technical owner: Platform Solutions (Diego Martinez)
- Security reviewer: Security & Compliance (Priya Nair)
- Escalation path: For pricing escalations contact Deal Desk (deal-desk@redwood.ai). For technical escalations escalate to Platform SRE oncall and CC the partner success manager.

Appendix: sample objection handling lines (short):
- "We already have a cloud endpoint": Redwood reduces operational burden and offers predictable SLOs plus a consistent API across deployment modes — you get the benefits of both managed endpoints and private control.
- "We need lower cost": Start with Redwood Optimize recommendations (quantization + batching) — partners typically see 30–60% per-token cost reductions depending on workload.

Last updated notes:
- 2029-01-10: Added pricing scenario for regulated Private deployments and updated pilot guidance.
- 2028-11-20: Initial draft and competitive matrix added.

Contact for feedback: partnerships@redwood.ai

title: Dealcraft: Engineering Conversation Compendium & Security FAQ
preview:
Summary:
This compendium is an engineer-first sales reference for pre-sales, solutions engineers, and account teams. It combines: concise positioning, conversation scripts tuned for technical audiences, competitive talking points, three pragmatic pricing scenarios with unit economics, a compact case study narrative suitable for slide inclusion, and a targeted security FAQ for technical and security reviewers. Use the conversation scripts verbatim or adapt as appropriate for the buyer persona.

1) One‑paragraph positioning (lead with what engineers care about)
Redwood Inference is a production-first LLM inference platform that gives platform and ML engineers consistent latency, predictable unit cost, and deployment flexibility (hosted, dedicated, private) without rebuilding the serving stack. We pair a GPU-optimized runtime (continuous batching, KV cache control, quantization profiles) with a unified control plane for routing, rollouts, and observability so teams hit latency, cost, and reliability targets faster.

2) Quick value bullets for technical audiences (elevator bullets)
- Predictable tail latency at p95/p99 via smart routing and autoscaling policies.
- 20–60% token-cost reductions on typical chat workloads by combining prefix caching and quantization profiles (customer-observed range).
- Single API and rollout model across hosted, dedicated, and private (reduces engineering maintenance).
- First-class observability: token-level cost breakdowns, per-model latency heatmaps, and prompt-set regression alerts.

3) Conversation scripts (engineer → engineer)
- Opening (discovery-focused): "Walk me through the end-to-end path from user request to model output in your app — where do you see the most variability in latency or the highest token cost today?"
- Value framing: "If you need predictable p99 latency under load and want to avoid building batching/eviction/caching logic, Redwood gives you the runtime primitives and control plane so your team can focus on product logic, not serving plumbing."
- Cost objection: "Instead of comparing MSRP, let’s model a real workload. Give me your avg tokens per request and expected qps and I’ll show a 3-way comparison (open self-host, other managed provider, Redwood Dedicated) with real unit economics."
- Vendor lock-in concern: "We expose standard model versions, can pin to specific weights, and our routing policies support seamless fallback to compatible model variants. For Private deployments, the control plane can be customer-hosted to reduce control-plane lock-in concerns."

4) Technical demo narrative (5–7 minute walkthrough)
1. Show a quick sample: REST request to Redwood API returning a streaming response.
2. Open Console: highlight per-route latency breakdown and token charge visualization.
3. Explain a rollout: canary 1% traffic -> metrics check -> auto-increment with rollback window.
4. Toggle quantization profile and show simulated cost/latency delta.
5. Show fallbacks: primary model OOMs -> auto-fallback to quantized variant with preserved output schema.

5) Competitive brief (one-paragraph takes + short table)
- MajorCloudAI: Strong on ecosystem integrations and data services; weaker in continuous-batching optimizations and observability granularity. Good fit for customers prioritizing managed MLOps beyond inference.
- ModelServeCo: Focused on self-hosted runtimes; flexible but requires substantial engineering to hit production economics at scale.
- EdgeOps: Excellent at ultra-low-latency on-device inference; less strong for centralized multi-tenant cost optimizations and rollout tooling.

Competitive snapshot (concise):
| Competitor   | Strengths                              | Typical gap vs Redwood                   | When to recommend Redwood |
|--------------|----------------------------------------|------------------------------------------|----------------------------|
| MajorCloudAI | Broad managed infra & data services    | higher per-token cost, less batching     | When product needs predictable unit economics and observability |
| ModelServeCo  | Self-host control, open runtimes       | heavy engineering lift for autoscaling   | When customer wants production-grade runtime without building it |
| EdgeOps       | On-device latency & offline inference  | limited centralized cost and rollout tooling | When centralized routing/rollouts and cost reductions matter |

6) Pricing guidance + three sample scenarios (numbers are illustrative; always run an EA-specific model with Deal Engineering)
Guiding principles:
- Hosted: pay per token + embeddings. Fastest time-to-value.
- Dedicated: committed monthly capacity with discounted unit cost and guaranteed SLOs.
- Private: platform fee + optional support/maintenance for on-prem/VPC; quoted per-customer.

Scenario A — Search/chat assistant (SaaS, mid-market)
- Assumptions: 10k MAU, 15 qps peak, avg 350 tokens/request, 70% user messages are short (<=64 tokens), 30% long (>=512 tokens).
- Redwood Hosted estimate (monthly): tokens/month = 15 qps * 3600 * 24 * 30 * avg 350 ≈ 13.5B tokens.
- Typical pricing: $0.0006 per generation token (example) -> $8,100/month.
- Benefit callouts: Enable prefix caching reduces gen tokens by ~18% (savings ≈ $1,458/month). If moved to Dedicated with 6-month commit, unit cost drops 30% -> net $5,670/month.

Scenario B — High-throughput reranking (adtech)
- Assumptions: 500 rps during peak, avg 40 tokens/candidate, 5 candidates per request.
- Token volume triple; cost-sensitive.
- Recommendation: Redwood Dedicated with aggressive batching & quantized variants. Expected unit reductions 40–60% vs naive hosted calls.

Scenario C — Regulated enterprise (finance)
- Assumptions: 1M transactions/month, require VPC + KMS + audit logs and 99.9% uptime SLA.
- Pricing: Private deployment: one-time platform fee + annual support + consumption. Typical structure: platform fee (one-time) $125k, annual support 18% of platform fee, per-token at negotiated enterprise rate.
- Value: predictable latency SLOs, encryption with customer-managed keys, and SOC2/ISO-ready evidence.

7) Short case study (one-slide narrative, 3 bullets + results)
Customer: LexiBank (regional bank) — scenario: chatbot + document ingestion for fraud detection triage.
- Challenge: inconsistent tail latency under concurrent batch ingestion and strict data residency requirements.
- Redwood solution: Private deployment in customer VPC, implemented KV prefix caching for common prompts, rolled a quantized reranker for low-latency triage.
- Results (90-day): p99 latency improved from 3.2s to 0.9s; token unit cost dropped by 38%; compliance audit passed with zero findings related to key management.

8) Security & compliance FAQ (designed for prospect security reviewers)
Q: What certifications and audits does Redwood maintain?
A: Redwood maintains SOC 2 Type II and is in the process of ISO 27001 certification for the control plane. For Private deployments, customers can supply their own evidence packages and we support third-party attestation reviews.

Q: How is customer data protected in transit and at rest?
A: All data in transit is TLS 1.2+; at rest data is AES-256 encrypted. For Redwood Hosted, we use provider-managed KMS with the option for customer-managed KMS (bring-your-own key). Private deployments can be configured with HSM-backed key stores.

Q: Where is customer data stored (data residency)?
A: Hosted customer data can be region-pinned to AWS/GCP regions we operate. Dedicated and Private allow explicit region control and on-prem options. We provide data retention and deletion controls per contract.

Q: How are model weights handled and can customers bring their own models?
A: Customers can bring their own model weights; Redwood supports onboarding with validation and performance profiling. Model weights can be stored in customer-controlled stores for Private deployments; for Hosted we provide vetted model hosting and versioning controls.

Q: What controls exist to prevent model drift and regression?
A: Redwood Optimize includes prompt-set based monitoring and regression alerts; teams can configure automatic rollback policies and can pin models to a specific version until manual approval.

Q: Do you support customer-managed audit logs?
A: Yes — we expose detailed audit logs (API keys used, route, token counts, model versions, timestamps). For Private customers we offer a streaming audit log integration to SIEMs.

Q: What is your incident response posture?
A: We have documented IR runbooks, an on-call rotation for SRE, and an SLA-based incident escalation path for Dedicated/Private customers. We provide post-incident RCA and mitigation plans.

9) Common objections + scripted responses
- "Your pricing is higher than X": "Let’s model your actual workload together. We focus on unit economics and SLO guarantees — in many cases we save cost once prefix caching and dedicated capacity are applied."
- "We can self-host cheaper": "Self-hosting shifts cost to time-to-market and ops headcount; we can run a TCO comparison across 12–18 months that includes infra, SRE, and opportunity cost."
- "We don’t want provider lock-in": "We support pinning, model export, and private control plane deployments. We prioritize portable artifacts (model versioning, schemaed outputs) to lower switching cost."

10) Deal close checklist (technical gating items for contract)
- Confirm region and data residency requirements.
- Confirm KMS/HSM needs and key ownership model.
- Agree on SLOs (p95, p99 latency targets) and measurement windows.
- Confirm rollout plan and rollback criteria (canary % schedule).
- Agree on retention windows and log streaming for SIEM.
- Define support SLOs and escalation contacts.

11) Appendix — quick CLI examples and JSON snippets (for engineering reviewers)
- Sample request (curl):
  curl -X POST https://api.redwood-inference.example/v1/generate 
    -H 'Authorization: Bearer <token>' 
    -H 'Content-Type: application/json' 
    -d '{"model":"redwood-chat-1", "input":"Summarize the following...", "stream":true}'

- Sample rollout policy (YAML excerpt):
  policy:
    name: canary-chat-rollout
    steps:
      - percent: 1
        duration: 30m
      - percent: 10
        duration: 1h
      - percent: 50

Use guidance: Always run a workload-based pricing simulation with Deal Engineering before final pricing. Update the token-pricing numbers in the Pricing playbook spreadsheet (Sales Drive) when a customer publishes real workloads.

Document ownership and updates:
Owner: Samira Chen (go-to-market) — update cadence: quarterly or after any material pricing/feature change.

Related materials and assets (links to slide decks, pricing calculators, demo recordings):
- Technical demo recording: (internal link)
- Pricing spreadsheet (Sales Drive): (internal link)
- Sample private deployment architecture diagram: (internal link)

title: Commercial Dossier for Technical Partners — Pricing, Messaging, Case Studies, & Security FAQ
preview:
Summary
-------
This dossier is a single-page commercial reference for technical partners (system integrators, ISVs, and channel partners) who co-sell or embed Redwood Inference. It bundles: concise positioning and messaging tailored to technical buyers, a short competitive brief, partner-facing pricing scenarios, a turn-key pilot and onboarding checklist, an outline for a partner-facing pitch deck, two anonymized case narratives, and a curated security FAQ that frequently appears during partner diligence. Use this during joint sales calls, partner enablement sessions, and pilot scoping.

Quick takeaways
- Core value proposition for partners: deliver predictable, production-ready LLM inference to customers while minimizing ops burden and preserving cost controls.
- Primary partner win conditions: predictable latency, controllable unit economics, auditability for regulated data, and simple integration paths (API + SDKs + DevOps automations).
- Pilot success metric (joint): demonstrate a production integration that meets customer SLOs, shows >=25% cost improvement vs current serving approach (or clear path to), and obtains one logged business outcome (e.g., time saved per user, reduction in human review).

Positioning & Messaging (technical buyer focus)
------------------------------------------------
Suggested short pitches (30–60s):
- For Platform Engineers: "Redwood gives you a single inference runtime that you can run as a hosted API, reserved Dedicated pools, or a private VPC. You get consistent latency, predictable throughput, and the operational tooling to measure and control token costs—so you can ship LLM features without owning fleet complexity."
- For ML/Model Ops: "Redwood’s serving runtime manages batching, KV cache, and quantization profiles with transparent performance tradeoffs. You can pin model versions, run canaries, and detect quality regressions before rollout."
- For Security/Compliance stakeholders: "We provide tenant isolation on Dedicated, key management (KMS/HSM) integrations for Private deployments, and audit logs that map tokens and requests to customer IDs for compliance evidence."

Message bank (talking points to reuse in calls)
- Predictability: Transparent model performance profiles and per-token cost drivers.
- Portability: Same API and rollout semantics across Hosted, Dedicated, and Private.
- Cost controls: Intelligent batching, prefix/KV caching, and quantization recommendations.
- Reliability: Automated routing and fallback to compatible variants to keep apps online.
- Observability: Per-model dashboards, token-level traces, and rollback hooks.

Competitive brief (concise)
-------------------------
Competitor archetypes to prepare for: managed cloud endpoint vendors, vertical LLM API providers, and in-house self-hosting.
- Managed cloud endpoints (large cloud provider): strengths: deep cloud integrations, software ecosystem; weaknesses vs Redwood: opaque inference economics, limited model catalog choices, and less prescriptive performance optimizations for LLM-specific runtimes.
- Vertical LLM API providers: strengths: domain-specific models, out-of-the-box prompts; weaknesses: limited deployment flexibility, poor observability for model regressions, harder to host on private infra.
- Self-host / Build-your-own: strengths: max control, potentially lower long-run cost at scale; weaknesses: high engineering overhead, slow iteration, and risk of regressions without evaluation harnesses.

Competitive rebuttal snippets
- If they say "we can self-host cheaper": "At small-to-medium scale we routinely see hidden ops and tuning costs; our runtime + tooling reduces total cost of ownership by reducing human tuning cycles and automating batching, caching, and quantization recommendations."
- If they ask about vendor lock-in: "We expose standard model artifacts and encourage pinned model versions. Customers can export model configs and workloads—our value is in serving economics and operational maturity, not opaque formats."

Pricing guidance for partner conversations
-----------------------------------------
Use these scenarios as conversation anchors; final pricing is customer-specific and should be approved by commercial. The numbers below are illustrative and reflect typical configurations used in partner pilots.

Pricing scenarios (illustrative)
| Scenario | Deployment | Typical monthly commit | Representative unit economics | Notes |
|---|---:|---:|---:|---|
| Small integration pilot | Hosted API | $2k–$10k | ~$3.50 per 1M input tokens; $7 per 1M output tokens | Short-term proof of concept, no infra changes |
| High-throughput SaaS feature | Dedicated (reserved) | $20k–$100k | Effective unit cost may fall by 3x vs hosted after autoscale and batching | Reserved GPU pools, long-running commit |
| Regulated enterprise | Private (VPC/On-prem) | $50k+ platform + support | Platform fee + infra; usage metered internally | Includes KMS integration and audit retention |

Up-sell levers to highlight to partners
- Dedicated capacity for predictable per-request latency and predictable unit economics.
- Observe-addons: extended retention for logs/traces, advanced evals retention, and custom quantization/benchmarking engagements.
- Professional services: model onboarding, prompt engineering, and reliability tuning.

Pilot & Integration Checklist (partner-facing, 8 steps)
-----------------------------------------------------
1) Pre-call: capture customer goals, throughput expectations (RPS), 95th pct latency SLO, and regulatory constraints (data residency, log retention).
2) Architecture sketch: agree on Hosted vs Dedicated vs Private. Capture VPC peering, egress rules, and KMS needs.
3) Data/Prompt selection: partner and customer pick a canonical prompt set and 50–200 evaluation examples.
4) Instrumentation plan: turn on per-route tracing, token cost dashboards, and evaluation hooks.
5) Pilot contract: commit level, timebox (30–90 days), success metrics (latency, cost per 1k tokens, business outcome).
6) Onboarding: API keys, SDK integration (node/python), sample ingesters, and CI pipeline hook for rollout.
7) Run pilot: daily synthesis, weekly checkpoint with quant metrics, one mid-pilot rollback test.
8) Graduation: conduct handoff, agree SLA tier, and document routing/failover policies.

Suggested integration scripts and CLI snippets
- Quick SDK call (Node.js):
  const Redwood = require('@redwood/inference');
  const client = new Redwood({ apiKey: process.env.RW_API_KEY });
  const r = await client.generate({ model: 'rwd-small-1', prompt: 'Summarize customer ticket', maxTokens: 150 });
  // capture r.latency_ms and r.token_usage for pilot dashboard

Pitch deck outline (partner-facing)
----------------------------------
1) Title / joint logo slide (1)
2) Problem statement / customer pain (1)
3) Joint solution overview (technical architecture diagram) (1)
4) Live demo / telemetry screenshots (2)
5) Economics snapshots (illustrative pricing scenarios + cost savings) (1)
6) Security & compliance (KMS, audit, data residency) (1)
7) Pilot scope & success metrics (1)
8) Commercial terms & next steps (1)
Notes: prepare a single-slide appendix with model performance profiles (latency by seq length, throughput by GPU type) that can be shared with SRE teams.

Two short anonymized partner case narratives (use in sales narratives)
---------------------------------------------------------------
Case A — Enterprise knowledge base search (integrator + Redwood)
- Customer: Large financial services firm (anonymized).
- Challenge: Customer's in-house model endpoints had inconsistent latency under peak loads and opaque cost per query.
- Approach: Partner integrated Redwood Dedicated with autoscaling pools, enabled prefix caching for common queries, and pinned model version for canary testing.
- Results: 45% reduction in average token cost, 2x improvement in 95th percentile latency, and production rollout across two business units in 8 weeks.

Case B — Regulated healthcare triage assistant (Redwood + Partner)
- Customer: Regional health system required private deployment and HSM-backed keys.
- Challenge: Audit trails and patient-data isolation; model updates needed staged rollouts.
- Approach: Private deployment inside customer VPC, KMS-integrated keys, evaluation harness to detect QA regressions, and canary rollouts for model updates.
- Results: Met regulatory audit requirements, reduced manual triage by 28%, and achieved a signed 12-month Dedicated commitment.

Security FAQ (partner diligence edition)
--------------------------------------
Q: Where are customer inputs and outputs stored?
A: By default, Hosted API stores short-term operational logs and traces for 7 days. For Dedicated and Private, storage policies are controlled by the customer; we offer configurable retention windows and can disable retention during pilot if required.

Q: What encryption options do you support for keys?
A: We support cloud-provider KMS (AWS KMS, GCP KMS), and HSM-backed solutions for Private deployments. Keys can be customer-managed; Redwood supports envelope encryption for transport and at-rest storage where applicable.

Q: How do you provide audit evidence for compliance reviews?
A: We provide request-level audit logs containing timestamps, route/model, token counts, request IDs, and hashed user identifiers. For Private deployments we can ship logs to customer SIEMs or configure S3/GCS exports.

Q: How do you handle model quality regressions?
A: We tie model rollouts to evaluation suites and alert on data drift or regressed prompt sets. Rollouts default to canary (5–20%) then staged ramps, and we provide automated rollback hooks if regression metrics exceed thresholds.

Q: What are SLA/operational targets partners can promise customers?
A: Standard SLAs by deployment type are: Hosted — uptime 99.9% platform-wide, Dedicated — customizable SLA (typically 99.95% with committed capacity), Private — governed by deployment contract. Typical latency targets are negotiated by workload; example target for interactive text generation is 95th pct <200 ms for small responses on Dedicated.

Q: How is customer data isolated in multi-tenant environments?
A: Tenant isolation occurs at three layers: logical request routing and per-tenant cache namespace, workload isolation in Dedicated pools, and network isolation via VPC for Private. We also provide RBAC for Console and API key scopes.

Operational & escalation play (partner responsibilities)
-----------------------------------------------------
- Redwood responsibilities: runtime availability, maintenance windows scheduling, security patching for managed control plane, and providing production telemetry.
- Partner responsibilities: integration, onboarding scripts, customer success during pilot, and first-line support to the customer.
- Escalation path: 1) Partner SRE -> Redwood Partner SE (email/escalation) -> Redwood SRE on-call -> Engineering pager. Include joint runbook link in the partner contract.

Appendix: Suggested pilot SLA template (excerpt)
------------------------------------------------
- Pilot window: 60 days from onboarding completion.
- Availability target during pilot: 99.5% for Hosted pilots; Dedicated pilots inherit reserved capacity uptime.
- Success criteria: 95th percentile latency within 2x production goal, token unit economics within agreed variance, and at least one validated business outcome.

Appendix: Owner matrix
- Redwood lead: Partner Solutions Engineer (owner: product enablement)
- Partner lead: Partner success manager (owner: partner org)
- Commercial owner: Channel Sales Manager

How to use this dossier in the field
-----------------------------------
- Pre-call: load the Pitch deck outline and tailor the architecture slide.
- Kickoff: agree success metrics and instrument the evaluation harness.
- Weekly syncs: share token usage and latency charts; call out anomalies earlier than technical buyers expect.

Contact and next steps
----------------------
- Redwood Partner SEs: partner-se@redwood.ai (request enablement lab access)
- For commercial approval: channel-sales@redwood.ai
- To request a dedicated pilot configuration: pilot-config@redwood.ai

Related internal references
- See our internal "Platform adoption roadmap" for partner motion triggers and discount thresholds.
- See eng-private-deployments deployment guides for VPC and on-prem packaging details.

Document history
----------------
- Created by Priya Natarajan (go-to-market) 2027-03-02.
- Reviewed by Liam O'Connor and Maya Chen 2027-04-15.
- Updated with additional pricing scenarios and SLA excerpt 2027-06-12.
text:
Summary
-------
This dossier is a single-page commercial reference for technical partners (system integrators, ISVs, and channel partners) who co-sell or embed Redwood Inference. It bundles: concise positioning and messaging tailored to technical buyers, a short competitive brief, partner-facing pricing scenarios, a turn-key pilot and onboarding checklist, an outline for a partner-facing pitch deck, two anonymized case narratives, and a curated security FAQ that frequently appears during partner diligence. Use this during joint sales calls, partner enablement sessions, and pilot scoping.

Quick takeaways
- Core value proposition for partners: deliver predictable, production-ready LLM inference to customers while minimizing ops burden and preserving cost controls.
- Primary partner win conditions: predictable latency, controllable unit economics, auditability for regulated data, and simple integration paths (API + SDKs + DevOps automations).
- Pilot success metric (joint): demonstrate a production integration that meets customer SLOs, shows >=25% cost improvement vs current serving approach (or clear path to), and obtains one logged business outcome (e.g., time saved per user, reduction in human review).

Positioning & Messaging (technical buyer focus)
------------------------------------------------
Suggested short pitches (30–60s):
- For Platform Engineers: "Redwood gives you a single inference runtime that you can run as a hosted API, reserved Dedicated pools, or a private VPC. You get consistent latency, predictable throughput, and the operational tooling to measure and control token costs—so you can ship LLM features without owning fleet complexity."
- For ML/Model Ops: "Redwood’s serving runtime manages batching, KV cache, and quantization profiles with transparent performance tradeoffs. You can pin model versions, run canaries, and detect quality regressions before rollout."
- For Security/Compliance stakeholders: "We provide tenant isolation on Dedicated, key management (KMS/HSM) integrations for Private deployments, and audit logs that map tokens and requests to customer IDs for compliance evidence."

Message bank (talking points to reuse in calls)
- Predictability: Transparent model performance profiles and per-token cost drivers.
- Portability: Same API and rollout semantics across Hosted, Dedicated, and Private.
- Cost controls: Intelligent batching, prefix/KV caching, and quantization recommendations.
- Reliability: Automated routing and fallback to compatible variants to keep apps online.
- Observability: Per-model dashboards, token-level traces, and rollback hooks.

Competitive brief (concise)
-------------------------
Competitor archetypes to prepare for: managed cloud endpoint vendors, vertical LLM API providers, and in-house self-hosting.
- Managed cloud endpoints (large cloud provider): strengths: deep cloud integrations, software ecosystem; weaknesses vs Redwood: opaque inference economics, limited model catalog choices, and less prescriptive performance optimizations for LLM-specific runtimes.
- Vertical LLM API providers: strengths: domain-specific models, out-of-the-box prompts; weaknesses: limited deployment flexibility, poor observability for model regressions, harder to host on private infra.
- Self-host / Build-your-own: strengths: max control, potentially lower long-run cost at scale; weaknesses: high engineering overhead, slow iteration, and risk of regressions without evaluation harnesses.

Competitive rebuttal snippets
- If they say "we can self-host cheaper": "At small-to-medium scale we routinely see hidden ops and tuning costs; our runtime + tooling reduces total cost of ownership by reducing human tuning cycles and automating batching, caching, and quantization recommendations."
- If they ask about vendor lock-in: "We expose standard model artifacts and encourage pinned model versions. Customers can export model configs and workloads—our value is in serving economics and operational maturity, not opaque formats."

| Regulated enterprise | Private (VPC/On-prem) | $50k+ platform + support | Platform fee + infra; usage metered internally | Includes KMS integration and audit retention |

title: Sales Architect Rapid Deck & CasePack — Positioning, Competitive Brief, Pricing Guidance, Case Study, and Security FAQ
preview:
Overview

Purpose: This single-page deliverable is a compact, ready-to-present kit for Sales Architects and Solutions Engineers to use in discovery, technical demos, and negotiation calls. It bundles concise positioning, battle-tested win themes, a short competitive brief, three pricing scenarios with math, a one-paragraph customer case study, a pilot-to-production checklist, and a security FAQ tailored for prospect technical reviewers. Use this when you have 30–60 minutes with an engineer, platform lead, or security reviewer.

How to use this pack

1) Start with the 2-slide narrative (covered in "Deck outline"). 2) Run a 10-minute demo focusing on the model routing and cost controls. 3) Present the pricing scenarios and recommend a pilot path. 4) Close technical questions with the Security FAQ and the Pilot Checklist.

Core positioning and win themes

- Developer-first inference platform that prioritizes predictable economics and production reliability.
- Unified runtime across hosted, dedicated, and private deployments — same API and observability regardless of where models run.
- Cost controls: intelligent batching, prefix/KV caching, and quantization recommendations that reduce per-token costs while preserving quality.
- Reliability: automated fallbacks (model variants, regions, capacity tiers) and SLA-backed Dedicated options for enterprise needs.
- Operational visibility: token-level cost, per-route latency breakdowns, and rollout controls for safe rollouts and fast triage.

Value statements for buyers

- For Platform Engineers: "Ship consistent inference with a single API and then tune economics centrally."
- For ML Engineers: "Keep model evaluation and rollout safe — Redwood handles serving performance and KV cache reuse so you can iterate models faster."
- For Security/Compliance: "Private deployments with customer-controlled networking and KMS/HSM integrations — audit logs are first-class."

Competitive brief (3-minute view)

Summary: Competitors range from public LLM APIs to self-hosted runtimes. Redwood wins when customers need production-grade SLAs, predictable unit economics, and a unified operational model across hybrid deployments.

Key competitive differentiators

- vs. general cloud-managed endpoints: Faster TTV and model catalog tuned for inference + prebuilt fallback policies.
- vs. single-vendor LLM APIs: Transparent performance profiles and better cost controls via batching/caching.
- vs. self-hosted runtimes: We provide orchestration, routing policies, and SRE-grade telemetry out-of-the-box.

Typical competitive objections and counter-frames

1) "We can host cheaper ourselves." → Counter: Provide 3-scenario economic model (below) showing total cost of ownership including SRE time, forecasting error, wasted capacity, and regression risk.
2) "Vendor lock-in risk." → Counter: Highlight unified API, ability to pin model versions, and exportable rollout configs; private deployments remove shared control plane concerns.
3) "Quality/regression safety." → Counter: Redwood Optimize + evals + per-prompt quality monitoring with regression alerts.

Pricing guidance & scenarios (example math)

Assumptions: 1M generated tokens/month; 200k embedding units/month; latency SLO <= 300 ms p50 for interactive routes.

Scenario A — Proof-of-concept (Pilot)
- Hosted API, Pay-as-you-go.
- @ 1M tokens: $0.25 / 1K tokens = $250. Embeddings: $0.03 / unit => $6k? (see math correction below)
- Recommended: 6-week pilot, $5k credit + technical onboarding.

Scenario B — Mid-market committed (Predictable)
- Dedicated small pool (reserved): baseline Uptime SLA + burst policy.
- Commit: $6k / month for reserved capacity that covers 1M tokens/month typical; effective blended token cost = $0.12 / 1K tokens.
- Add-ons: observability retention + dedicated support = $1k / month.

Scenario C — Enterprise Private (Compliance-focused)
- On-prem/VPC private deployment with platform license and support. Typical contract: $80k annual platform fee + $X infra costs (customer).
- Includes deployment packaging, HSM/KMS integration, audit log retention config, and on-site enablement.

Note on math: These scenarios are templates — always run the deal economics calculator in the Deal Engineer toolkit and replace placeholder unit prices with current rate card. Use the token breakdown from the demo to size the dedicated pool.

Pilot-to-production checklist (technical and commercial)

1) Discovery: Confirm throughput needs (rps and tokens/session distribution), compliance requirements, and third-party network constraints.
2) Pilot design: Select a single route + two model variants; enable KV caching and streaming.
3) Bench: Run synthetic load at 2x expected peak for 24 hours; capture p50/p90 latencies and cost per 10k tokens.
4) Instrument: Hook Redwood Console dashboards, add tracing hooks, and upload a set of guardrail prompts for quality monitoring.
5) Commercial: Align billing model (PayG vs reserved), SLA expectations, and draft renewal/scale triggers.

One-paragraph customer case study (condensed)

Acme Retail (mid-market, multi-region): Replaced an ad-hoc mixture of cloud endpoints and home-grown inference serving. Outcome after 90 days: 35% lower per-token cost via caching and batching, 40% reduction in tail latency for shopping assistant flows (p95 from 850ms to 520ms), and an operationally simpler deployment with a single dashboard for model rollouts. Contract expanded to Dedicated within 6 months.

Security FAQ for technical reviewers (top 10 quick answers)

Q1. Can we deploy Redwood in our VPC or on-prem?
A1. Yes — Redwood Private supports VPC and on-prem packages with a customer-controlled control plane and optional air-gapped deployment.

Q2. How are encryption keys managed?
A2. Integrations: cloud KMS (AWS/GCP/Azure) and HSM options supported. Keys can be customer-managed; we do not persist plaintext keys.

Q3. Is customer data logged?
A3. Audit logging is configurable. By default, core operational metadata is logged; payload logging is off for Private deployments unless explicitly enabled for troubleshooting under an NDA.

Q4. What compliance evidence do you provide?
A4. SOC2 Type II report, penetration test summary, and architecture diagrams for private deployments. For customers with HIPAA needs we provide a BAA template and deployment checklist.

Q5. How do you protect model outputs against data leakage?
A5. We provide model variant pinning, strict rollout controls, prompt/eval-based regression alerts, and input/output redaction options.

Q6. What's your incident response posture?
A6. Eng-SRE maintains an incident runbook with 24/7 paging for high-sev production incidents. Customers on enterprise SLAs receive a designated TAM and post-incident RCA within 3 business days.

Q7. Are there VPC peering or private link options?
A7. Yes — VPC peering, PrivateLink (AWS), and private interconnects (GCP/Azure) are supported for low-latency, secure traffic.

Q8. How is customer isolation guaranteed on Dedicated?
A8. Dedicated capacity provides isolated GPU pools, tenant-level quotas, and network segmentation. Control plane metadata can be restricted per-customer.

Q9. Can we bring our own model weights?
A9. In most cases yes: Redwood supports customer-supplied models with a model onboarding process and verification runbook (performance and security checks). Private deployments are preferred for sensitive IP.

Q10. What are typical breach response commitments?
A10. For enterprise contracts we include notification timelines, RMS contact list, and collaboration commitments for remediation and forensics.

Appendix: 12-slide deck outline (two-slide quick start)

- Slide 1: Hook — "Make LLMs predictable: cost, latency, and reliability across any deployment." (1–2 bullets + logo)
- Slide 2: Architecture sketch — unified API + routing + caching + hosting modes (visual).
- Slide 3: Three core win themes (developer velocity, economics, reliability).
- Slide 4: Demo snapshot — per-route latency and token cost chart (screenshot).
- Slide 5: Pricing scenarios (compressed table).
- Slide 6: Pilot plan and timelines.
- Slide 7: Security & compliance highlights.
- Slide 8: Customer case study (metrics and quote).
- Slide 9: Competitive positioning matrix (one-liner per competitor).
- Slide 10: Implementation plan & handoffs.
- Slide 11: SLAs, support, and escalation path.
- Slide 12: Next steps & champion asks (trial activation, TAM intro).

Owner, escalation, and next steps

- Owner: Nina Alvarez (Sales Architecture) — primary contact for slide customizations, live demo scripts, and pricing templates.
- Escalations: Marcus Lin (Senior SE) for technical deep dives; Priya Reddy (Legal) for contract security exceptions.
- Next steps: For an active opportunity, request a Deal Engineer run of the economics tool and book a 60-minute pilot planning workshop with Solutions Engineering.

Version history

- 2026-11-03: Draft created.
- 2027-01-12: Added competitive counters and revised pricing placeholders.
- 2027-02-14: Security FAQ updated; pilot checklist tightened.
