- Includes deployment packaging, HSM/KMS integration, audit log retention config, and on-site enablement.

Note on math: These scenarios are templates — always run the deal economics calculator in the Deal Engineer toolkit and replace placeholder unit prices with current rate card. Use the token breakdown from the demo to size the dedicated pool.

Pilot-to-production checklist (technical and commercial)

1) Discovery: Confirm throughput needs (rps and tokens/session distribution), compliance requirements, and third-party network constraints.
2) Pilot design: Select a single route + two model variants; enable KV caching and streaming.
3) Bench: Run synthetic load at 2x expected peak for 24 hours; capture p50/p90 latencies and cost per 10k tokens.
4) Instrument: Hook Redwood Console dashboards, add tracing hooks, and upload a set of guardrail prompts for quality monitoring.
5) Commercial: Align billing model (PayG vs reserved), SLA expectations, and draft renewal/scale triggers.

One-paragraph customer case study (condensed)

Acme Retail (mid-market, multi-region): Replaced an ad-hoc mixture of cloud endpoints and home-grown inference serving. Outcome after 90 days: 35% lower per-token cost via caching and batching, 40% reduction in tail latency for shopping assistant flows (p95 from 850ms to 520ms), and an operationally simpler deployment with a single dashboard for model rollouts. Contract expanded to Dedicated within 6 months.

Security FAQ for technical reviewers (top 10 quick answers)

Q1. Can we deploy Redwood in our VPC or on-prem?
A1. Yes — Redwood Private supports VPC and on-prem packages with a customer-controlled control plane and optional air-gapped deployment.

Q2. How are encryption keys managed?
A2. Integrations: cloud KMS (AWS/GCP/Azure) and HSM options supported. Keys can be customer-managed; we do not persist plaintext keys.

Q3. Is customer data logged?
A3. Audit logging is configurable. By default, core operational metadata is logged; payload logging is off for Private deployments unless explicitly enabled for troubleshooting under an NDA.

Q4. What compliance evidence do you provide?
A4. SOC2 Type II report, penetration test summary, and architecture diagrams for private deployments. For customers with HIPAA needs we provide a BAA template and deployment checklist.

Q5. How do you protect model outputs against data leakage?
A5. We provide model variant pinning, strict rollout controls, prompt/eval-based regression alerts, and input/output redaction options.

Q6. What's your incident response posture?
A6. Eng-SRE maintains an incident runbook with 24/7 paging for high-sev production incidents. Customers on enterprise SLAs receive a designated TAM and post-incident RCA within 3 business days.

Q7. Are there VPC peering or private link options?
A7. Yes — VPC peering, PrivateLink (AWS), and private interconnects (GCP/Azure) are supported for low-latency, secure traffic.

Q8. How is customer isolation guaranteed on Dedicated?
A8. Dedicated capacity provides isolated GPU pools, tenant-level quotas, and network segmentation. Control plane metadata can be restricted per-customer.

Q9. Can we bring our own model weights?
A9. In most cases yes: Redwood supports customer-supplied models with a model onboarding process and verification runbook (performance and security checks). Private deployments are preferred for sensitive IP.

Q10. What are typical breach response commitments?
A10. For enterprise contracts we include notification timelines, RMS contact list, and collaboration commitments for remediation and forensics.

Appendix: 12-slide deck outline (two-slide quick start)

- Slide 1: Hook — "Make LLMs predictable: cost, latency, and reliability across any deployment." (1–2 bullets + logo)
- Slide 2: Architecture sketch — unified API + routing + caching + hosting modes (visual).
- Slide 3: Three core win themes (developer velocity, economics, reliability).
- Slide 4: Demo snapshot — per-route latency and token cost chart (screenshot).
- Slide 5: Pricing scenarios (compressed table).
- Slide 6: Pilot plan and timelines.
- Slide 7: Security & compliance highlights.
- Slide 8: Customer case study (metrics and quote).
- Slide 9: Competitive positioning matrix (one-liner per competitor).
- Slide 10: Implementation plan & handoffs.
- Slide 11: SLAs, support, and escalation path.
- Slide 12: Next steps & champion asks (trial activation, TAM intro).

Owner, escalation, and next steps

- Owner: Nina Alvarez (Sales Architecture) — primary contact for slide customizations, live demo scripts, and pricing templates.
- Escalations: Marcus Lin (Senior SE) for technical deep dives; Priya Reddy (Legal) for contract security exceptions.
- Next steps: For an active opportunity, request a Deal Engineer run of the economics tool and book a 60-minute pilot planning workshop with Solutions Engineering.

Version history

- 2026-11-03: Draft created.
- 2027-01-12: Added competitive counters and revised pricing placeholders.
- 2027-02-14: Security FAQ updated; pilot checklist tightened.

title: Engineers' Toolbox: LLM Integration Scripts and CISO Evidence Companion
preview:
Summary:

This companion document is a compact, engineer-facing toolkit for sales engineers and platform owners evaluating Redwood Inference. It bundles: concise positioning and messaging for technical audiences; a competitive snapshot focused on operational trade-offs; a recommended three-stage pilot-to-scale plan with telemetry thresholds and rollout steps; code snippets and integration examples (hosted + private); a pricing guidance cheat sheet for pilots and committed capacity; a short customer case study; and a CISO-friendly security FAQ with concrete evidence artifacts and owner contacts.

Audience and intent:
- Primary: Platform engineers, SREs, security reviewers, and sales engineers running technical evaluations.
- Secondary: Solution architects, procurement leads, and technical product managers.

1) Engineer-focused positioning (elevator + technical themes)

Elevator (for engineers):
Redwood Inference provides a single runtime that makes production LLM inference predictable and economical across hosted, reserved (Dedicated), and private deployments. It reduces per-token cost through automatic batching/caching, improves latency via hardware-aware kernel selection, and preserves safety with audit logs and deterministic fallbacks.

Key technical themes to lead with:
- Unified API and SDKs: same request semantics for hosted, dedicated, and private (minimizes integration surface).
- Deterministic performance controls: configurable batching/caching, quantization profiles, and autoscaling policies with observable unit economics.
- Graceful degradation: policy-driven routing and automatic fallback to lower-capacity model variants by latency/cost tiers.
- Security-by-design: VPC-private control plane, KMS integrations, and end-to-end audit logging for compliance checks.

2) Quick competitive snapshot (operational lens)

Competitors considered: major managed LLM APIs, generic cloud-managed endpoints, and self-hosted inference runtimes.

Differentiation matrix (operational priorities):
- Latency predictability: Redwood (high) | Generic APIs (variable) | Self-hosted (depends)
- Cost control per token: Redwood (high, explicit controls) | Generic APIs (moderate) | Self-hosted (high ops cost)
- Deployment flexibility: Redwood (hosted/dedicated/private) | Generic APIs (hosted) | Self-hosted (on-prem only)
- Enterprise evidence (SOCs, KMS): Redwood (SOC2 + KMS patterns) | Generic APIs (varies) | Self-hosted (customer-owned)

Operational trade-offs to call out:
- Self-hosted wins for absolute data control, but requires significant SRE investment for batching/kv/cache optimizations.
- Generic managed APIs are easiest to start with but can expose cost/latency unpredictability at scale.
- Redwood aims to reduce that operational burden while offering enterprise controls.

3) Three-stage pilot-to-scale playbook (engineer-friendly)

Goal: deliver a production-quality route from PoC to serving 10M tokens/day with <75ms P50 generation latency for short prompts (<=128 tokens) by the end of Stage 3.

Stage A — Rapid proof-of-concept (1–2 weeks)
- Scope: implement a single endpoint call (chat or generation) in a sandbox account with sample prompts.
- Success criteria: end-to-end request <200ms P50 (hosted), valid example responses for 95% of test prompts, and a basic security checklist signed off by security engineer.
- Deliverables: integration demo app, sample prompt set (50 prompts), cost estimate for expected per-token usage.
- Owner: Sales Engineer + Customer Platform Engineer.

Stage B — Pilot with observability (3–6 weeks)
- Scope: integrate Redwood Console observability hooks, enable token-level cost reporting, configure a canary route (5% traffic).
- Success criteria: cost per 1k tokens within target (example: <= $1.20 / 1k tokens for medium prompt lengths when using curated model X-q4), latency SLO met (P95 < 350ms), and end-to-end tracing present for 100% of errors.
- Ramp steps: 1) shift 5% traffic, 2) validate logs & metrics for 7 days, 3) run adversarial prompt set and evaluate responses vs baseline.
- Deliverables: tracing dashboard, regression report, cost/billing export, rollback runbook.
- Owner: Customer Platform Engineering & Redwood Customer Success.

Stage C — Production rollout (4–12 weeks)
- Scope: Dedicated capacity or Private deployment as required; configure autoscale and fallback policies; run a 30-day burn demonstrating SLO attainment.
- Success criteria: sustained throughput matches SLAs (e.g., 100 qps sustained on a 4-GPU Dedicated pool), error budget <1% monthly, and documented security evidence for procurement.
- Deliverables: Dedicated pool sizing spec, autoscaling policy YAML, quantization profile recommendation, post-mortem on pilot anomalies.
- Owner: Customer SRE + Redwood Dedicated Ops.

4) Observability & telemetry checklist (required for pilot acceptance)
- Token-level cost reporting enabled (Console export + S3 export).
- Latency by route: P50, P95, P99 for generation and streaming.
- Error class breakdown: rate-limited, model-unavailable, decode errors, network errors.
- Trace IDs correlated with platform logs for triage (linkable to request_id).
- Drift monitoring: weekly prompt set pass/fail and MRR for quality regressions.

5) Pricing guidance cheat sheet (pilot vs committed)

Pilot (recommended):
- Free trial credits + usage-based billing: estimate pilot cost with formula: expected_tokens_per_day * days * pilot_unit_cost
- Example: 500k tokens/day * 14 days * $0.0007/token = $4,900 (one-time pilot consumption)

Committed / Dedicated guidance (example ranges):
- Small reserved pool (2 x A100 equivalent): $30k–$50k/month, target throughput ~150–300 qps depending on model and sequence length.
- Medium reserved pool (4 x A100 equivalent): $55k–$95k/month, target throughput ~300–700 qps.
- Private on-prem licensing: one-time platform fee + support contract; licensing varies by customer scale — typical starting TCV for midmarket $250k/year.

Negotiation levers for deals:
- Burst capacity add-on: available for Dedicated to handle 30–50% peak spikes for a premium.
- Longer-term commits reduce unit cost and improve model variant availability.
- Observability & retention add-ons for enterprise compliance (longer log retention, full trace retention).

6) Integration examples (copy-paste friendly)

Hosted API: simple streaming generation (curl)

curl -s -N https://api.redwood.ai/v1/generate \\
  -H "Authorization: Bearer $REDWOOD_API_KEY" \\
  -H "Content-Type: application/json" \\
  -d '{"model":"rwd-neo-medium-2027","input":"Summarize the following conversation...","max_tokens":128,"stream":true}'

Hosted SDK (python, minimal):

from redwood import RedwoodClient
client = RedwoodClient(api_key=os.environ['REDWOOD_API_KEY'])
resp = client.generate(model='rwd-neo-medium-2027', input='Hello world', max_tokens=64)
print(resp.text)

Private deployment sample (k8s values snippet):

# values.yaml (snippet)
replicaCount: 3
nodeSelector:
  kubernetes.io/instance-type: gpu-rtx8000
resources:
  limits:
    nvidia.com/gpu: 1
modelCache:
  enabled: true
  sizeGb: 64
quantizationProfile: q4_0
networking:
  controlPlaneCIDR: 10.0.0.0/24
  allowedEgress: ["s3://redwood-artifacts", "api.redwood.ai"]

Config-as-code example (autoscale policy YAML):

policyName: latency-aware-autoscale
minReplicas: 2
maxReplicas: 12
targetP95LatencyMs: 300
scaleUpStep: 1
scaleDownCooldownSec: 600

7) Sample acceptance checklist for procurement & security
- SOC2 Type II report available (attach latest artifact).
- Penetration test summary and remediation log (last 12 months).
- KMS integration guide and key rotation cadence.
- Data flow diagram showing PII ingress, processing, and retention points.
- Audit log retention options and export mechanism (S3 / SIEM).
- SLAs and outage RTO commitments for Dedicated.

8) Customer case vignette (BrightCart e-commerce)

Context: BrightCart is a mid-market e-commerce platform that deployed Redwood Dedicated to replace a mixture of host-model endpoints and in-house inference.
Outcome summary (90-day):
- 60% reduction in per-conversation inference cost after moving to batching + q4 quantized model for short queries.
- Average latency P95 reduced from 520ms to 260ms on product search reranking.
- One-month pilot led to a committed 6-month Dedicated contract.

Key instrumentation used: token-level billing exports, canary route (10% traffic), and week-over-week quality regressions reported to product via the Console.

9) Security FAQ for prospects (CISO-friendly answers)

Q: Where is my data stored and is data retained?
A: For hosted API, customer request payloads are stored for up to 30 days for quality/regression monitoring by default and can be configured per-account. For Dedicated and Private, customers control retention policy; retention can be set to 0 days and logs exported to customer S3/KMS.

Q: What controls exist for encryption and key management?
A: We support customer-managed keys via AWS KMS or HSM-backed key stores for Dedicated and Private control planes. TLS 1.3 enforced for in-transit; AES-256 for at-rest.

Q: Can Redwood operate in an air-gapped environment?
A: Yes — our Private on-prem package includes an installer and attestable binary set. We run an airgap validation checklist and work with the customer's security team for attestation and smoke tests.

Q: What compliance evidence is available?
A: SOC2 Type II report, ISO 27001 scope summary (select customers), vulnerability management policies, and supply-chain security statement. Specific artifacts are shared under NDA.

Q: How do you handle model hallucinations and guardrails?
A: We provide structured output schemas, response filters, and reranking integrations. Customers can attach deterministic filters and fallback handlers in the platform routing policies. We surface quality regressions via the Console and support custom eval sets.

Q: What is the incident response and notification policy?
A: Critical incidents (P1: platform-wide outage) trigger immediate pager notifications to customers with subscription to status updates. Post-incident reports are shared within 72 hours and include root cause, impact, and remediation steps. Dedicated customers may opt into a 24/7 escalation channel.

Q: Who owns security support during pilot?
A: Primary contact is Redwood Customer Security Liaison (security-liaison@redwood.ai). For Dedicated/Private projects, a named Solutions Security Engineer is assigned during Stage B.

10) Appendix: copyable artifact links and owner contacts
- Example prompt set (50 prompts): /internal-assets/prompt-sets/quickship-50.json (sales access only)
- SOC2 report (redacted): /legal-artifacts/soc2/redwood-soc2-2026-redacted.pdf (NDA required)
- Contact roster: Maya Chen (SE lead) maya.chen@redwood.ai; Diego Alvarez (Solutions Engineering) diego.alvarez@redwood.ai; Security liaison: security-liaison@redwood.ai

How to use this file in customer conversations:
1) Start with the elevator and the three technical themes to align with platform engineers.
2) Run the Stage A PoC with the hosted curl example and capture baseline cost/latency.
3) If pilot criteria are met, promote to Stage B and enable Console telemetry; provide the procurement checklist to legal/security.
4) Use the pricing guidance to provide a near-term conservative quote and table the dedicated capacity options for engineering review.

Change history:
- 2026-11-07: Created by Maya Chen.
- 2027-02-10: Added Dedicated sizing guidance and new Hosted API curl example.

Owners and escalation:
- Primary owner: Maya Chen (Sales Engineering).
- Security reviewer and escalation: Priya Natarajan (Security & Compliance).
- Legal/procurement escalation: procurement@redwood.ai

End of document.

title: AWS Marketplace offer preflight playbook (2026): launch checklist + hero copy templates
preview:
h1. Purpose
This playbook is the standard preflight packet for an AWS Marketplace offer (new listing or major revision). It covers:
- The required artifacts to unblock Procurement/Legal review
- Recommended SKU structure and metering defaults
- Approved hero copy templates (with compliance-safe language)
- A one-page co-sell blurb template for AWS Partner Ops

This page was created after repeated partner asks for a “short checklist + 2–3 hero copy options” to speed internal alignment.

h1. When to use
Use this playbook when:
- A partner requests an AWS Marketplace listing (public listing, private offer, or both)
- A deal is targeting AWS co-sell registration
- Procurement requests a “preflight checklist” before engaging Legal/Security

h1. Owners and turnaround expectations
- DRI (Partners/Marketplace): Partnerships Director (default)
- Messaging: Product Marketing
- Packaging/Metering: Platform GTM + Product
- Co-sell: Partner Ops enablement

SLA targets (typical):
1) Preflight checklist + draft hero copy within 2 business days
2) Consolidated partner packet within 3 business days

h1. Preflight packet contents (send as one consolidated PDF or folder)
Deliverables to include:
1) Offer preflight checklist (see below)
2) Hero copy options (2–3) + subheadline
3) SKU + metering summary (1 page)
4) Co-sell one-pager blurb (paragraph + value props + use cases)
5) Image guidance notes (or link to latest design guidance)

h1. Offer preflight checklist (required artifacts)
The table below is the canonical “what we need” list. If a partner asks for a checklist, copy/paste this section into email and offer to provide a filled-in version.

h2. Checklist table
| Category | Item | Required for | Notes / tips |
|---|---|---|---|
| Listing basics | Product name + short description | All offers | Keep description consistent with website naming; avoid unverified superlatives. |
| Listing basics | Category + keywords | All offers | Use stable keyword set across AWS + website + decks. |
| Packaging | SKU names + SKU descriptions | All offers | See recommended SKU set below; keep names stable for billing mappings. |
| Metering | Metering dimension(s) + unit definitions | SaaS offers | Prefer token-based metering where applicable; define rounding/minimums explicitly. |
| Pricing | Public price card | Public listing | Include any volume tiers and minimum monthly commits if applicable. |
| Pricing | Private Offer readiness | Enterprise deals | Confirm discount bands and approval path; define start/end dates and renewal term language. |
| Legal | EULA/contract addendum link | All offers | Confirm latest template; avoid partner-specific edits without Legal review. |
| Security | SOC 2 / ISO status statement | Most enterprise deals | Provide the “what we can share” summary and the evidence request process. |
| Support | Support tiers + response times | All offers | Provide business hours, P1 expectations, and escalation channel. |
| Images | Hero image(s) + logo usage approval | Public listing | Follow AWS image constraints; do not use customer logos without written permission. |
| Sales enablement | Value props (top 3) + ICP | Co-sell | Must map to measurable outcomes; avoid hard performance numbers unless approved. |
| Implementation | Getting started steps + prerequisites | All offers | Include IAM/network prerequisites and onboarding timeline ranges. |

h1. SKU and metering guidance (2026 standard)
This section supersedes older notes that used different metering for dedicated capacity.

h2. Recommended SKU set
Default recommendation is 3 SKUs (use 2 only if product scope is narrow):
- Standard (Shared hosted): best for trials, dev/test, and smaller workloads
- Dedicated (Reserved capacity): best for predictable production workloads needing isolation
- Enterprise (Private Offer only): used when commercial terms require custom commitments, add-ons, or bundled support

Important: Some partners will ask whether “standard/dedicated” is final so their billing team can map the catalog. For 2026, these names are the preferred defaults; only change if there is an existing contracted naming scheme.

h2. Metering and pricing defaults
- Primary usage metric (preferred): tokens-in/tokens-out (or “total tokens processed” if a single dimension is required)
- Dedicated capacity: prefer token-based metering plus a minimum monthly commit rather than vGPU-hour metering, unless Finance explicitly requires capacity-based billing for that product line
- Pricing footnotes to include:
  1) Clear definition of what counts as input vs output tokens
  2) Rounding behavior and any minimum billable unit
  3) Overages/burst behavior (if enabled)

h2. Private Offer guidance
- Default: enable Private Offer for initial enterprise customers to accelerate procurement and ensure predictable billing
- Include in partner packet:
  - Proposed term (12/24/36 months)
  - Discount band (if pre-approved)
  - Start date, renewal behavior, and any ramp schedule

h1. Hero copy templates (compliance-safe)
Keep hero copy short (target: <= 85 characters for the main line). Avoid unapproved hard claims (exact latency, “X% cheaper”) unless Product + Legal sign-off is recorded.

h2. Options (2026)
Option A — "LLM inference, production-grade: predictable latency and cost at scale."
Subline — "Run open and custom models—fast, reliable, and cloud-ready."

Option B — "Ship AI features with zero ops friction: high-throughput inference for teams."
Subline — "Dedicated capacity, private options, and integrated cost controls."

Option C — "Cut inference costs. Keep quality. Scale confidently."
Subline — "Unified runtime across hosted, dedicated, and private deployments."

Notes:
- Option B text reflects the preferred legal-friendly wording (“with zero ops friction”) and replaces older phrasing (“without the ops”).
- If a partner requests stronger performance language, route to Product for claim substantiation and approval.

h1. Co-sell blurb template (AWS Partner Ops)
Provide AWS Partner Ops a single paragraph plus 3 value props and 3 use cases.

h2. One-paragraph template
"Redwood Inference helps teams ship production AI features by providing reliable, cost-controlled model inference across shared hosted and dedicated capacity. Customers use Redwood to reduce operational overhead, improve predictability for latency-sensitive workloads, and scale usage with clear commercial terms through AWS Marketplace."

h2. Top 3 value props (edit to fit product scope)
- Predictable performance for production inference workloads
- Cost controls and transparent metering aligned to usage
- Flexible deployment options (shared, dedicated, private offer commercial paths)

h2. Top 3 use cases (choose the most relevant)
- Agentic workflows and RAG applications in customer support and knowledge search
- Real-time personalization and recommendation inference
- Internal copilots and document automation for operations teams

h1. Email-ready response (copy/paste)
Subject: AWS Marketplace preflight pack: checklist + hero copy + SKU guidance

Body:
"Thanks—attached is our AWS Marketplace offer preflight pack. It includes (1) the required artifacts checklist, (2) 3 hero copy options with sublines, (3) recommended SKU/metering defaults, and (4) a co-sell one-paragraph summary + value props/use cases. If Procurement/Legal needs MSA redlines or security evidence, tell us what format they prefer and we’ll prioritize."

h1. Change log
- 2026-12-10: Updated Option B hero copy to “with zero ops friction” and clarified SKU/metering default to prefer token-based metering for dedicated offers unless Finance requires capacity-based billing.
- 2026-11-05: Initial publication based on partner request pattern (checklist + 2–3 hero copy options + SKU guidance + co-sell blurb).

title: Pilot Signal-to-Renewal Playbook and Technical Risk Evidence
preview:
Overview:

This playbook is a pragmatic, field-ready reference for technical sales, solutions engineering, and account teams running inference pilots with Redwood. It focuses on the conversion path from pilot activation to renewal/contract, maps concrete operational signals that predict success, and bundles the technical evidence that risk-conscious buyers (platform, security, and infra teams) need to approve production deployments.

Audience:
- Sales engineers, account executives (AEs), solutions engineers (SEs)
- Customer platform/infra leads running pilots
- Customer procurement and security reviewers

Objectives:
1) Standardize pilot structure and success criteria so results are comparable across deals.
2) Reduce friction between pilot and procurement by pre-packaging technical evidence and controls.
3) Surface the signal set (metrics + behaviors) that reliably predicts renewal/expansion.

1) One-line positioning and talk tracks
- Elevator: \"Redwood Inference delivers predictable LLM inference economics and reliability across hosted, dedicated, and private deployments—so engineering teams ship features without building an inference stack.\"
- Platform technical owner: \"We bake in deterministic latency SLOs, workload-aware batching, and automated fallbacks so your production endpoints keep serving even when capacity shifts.\"
- Finance/CFO: \"Start with a pilot that proves unit-costs at production scale—our recommended test shows token-level costs and cache hit rates that map directly to your P&L assumptions.\"

2) Competitive brief (concise)
- Primary competitors to frame against: Managed cloud endpoints (major cloud AI services), single-vendor LLM APIs, open-source self-hosted runtimes with orchestration.
- Redwood differentiators: unified runtime across hosted/dedicated/private, cost-aware controls (batching/cache/quantization recommendations), clear SLOs and rollback policies, built-in canary and automatic fallback to compatible model variants.
- Typical competitor rebuttals:
  - \"Cloud provider endpoints are simpler.\" Rebuttal: For scale, provider variability, costs, and vendor-specific fallbacks increase risk—Redwood provides predictable throughput with explicit cost controls and transparent kernel/runtime profiles.
  - \"We can self-host to save money.\" Rebuttal: Self-hosted requires engineering time to build robust autoscaling, KV cache persistence, kernel selection, and monitoring—Redwood's Dedicated/Private options accelerate time-to-production with engineered SLOs and toolchains.

3) Pilot structure (recommended 6-week cadence)
- Week 0: Discovery & success criteria (OKRs)
  - Deliverables: pilot charter, test dataset, traffic profile (qps, seq length dist), rubric for quality (precision/recall or human evals), cost ceiling.
- Weeks 1–2: Integration & baseline runs
  - Deliverables: SDK integration, baseline latency and cost per model variant, initial monitoring dashboard with token-level cost breakdowns.
- Weeks 3–4: Optimization & stress tests
  - Deliverables: batch/caching config, quantization suggestions, dedicated capacity sizing, burst/scale tests, canary plan.
- Weeks 5–6: Hand-off & renewal negotiation
  - Deliverables: final report (metrics + runbook), pricing proposal (recommended capacity and transition path), SLAs and compliance annex.

4) Success criteria and leading signals (metrics + behavioral)
- Quantitative signals (primary):
  - 95th percentile latency <= agreed SLO (example SLO: 250ms for <1k qps pilot)
  - Cost per 1M tokens (post-optimization) <= pilot cost ceiling (customer-specific)
  - Cache hit rate (prefix/KV) >= 35% for repeat request patterns
  - Model quality regression <= 2% on agreed eval set
  - Error rate (5xx/timeout) < 0.5% sustained
- Qualitative/behavioral signals (leading indicators):
  - Product team executes 2+ distinct feature flows through Redwood endpoints during pilot
  - Platform/infra team requests rollout plan for Dedicated or Private deployment
  - Security team requests compliance-specific artifacts (audit logs, KMS integration) rather than rejecting due to open questions

5) Pricing guidance and conversion economics (templates)
- Pilot pricing templates (examples to share):
  1) Hosted trial: credits-based, includes 1M tokens + 50k embedding units, 30 days, support triage included.
  2) Dedicated pilot: 4-week reserved pool (1–2 instances equivalent), flat monthly pilot fee + overage per token beyond threshold.
  3) Private (VPC) evaluation: one-week PoC environment with reduced retention, per-day engineering seat for on-prem packaging.

- Example conversion calculator (text table):
| Metric | Pilot measurement | Conversion assumption | Annualized impact |
|---|---:|---:|---:|
| Tokens/day (peak) | 2,000,000 | 2x growth | 1.46B tokens/year |
| Cost/token (hosted, optimized) | $0.000002 | unchanged | $2,920/year |
| Expected cache reduction | 30% | improved to 40% | delta savings $1,168/year |

Note: Use the real-time pricing worksheet in the CloudQuote tool to compute committed tier vs on-demand tradeoffs.

6) Operational runbook snippets to include in pilot hand-off
- Canary policy example (text):
  - 10% traffic for 24 hours to new dedicated pool; 1-hour rollback window on error spike >2x baseline error rate.
- Autoscaling triggers (example thresholds):
  - Scale-up: 5-minute sustained CPU/GPU utilization > 75% or queue latency > 150ms
  - Scale-down: 10-minute sustained utilization < 30% and queue length < 5 requests.

7) Packaged technical evidence (what to attach to pilot proposals)
- Runtime performance profile: latency histograms (p50/p90/p95), tokens/sec, KV-cache hit heatmap
- Security artifacts: SOC-like checklist, KMS/HSM integration guide, audit log sample, data flow diagram (text)
- Compliance notes: GDPR data residency options, retention policy templates, encryption-at-rest and in-transit details
- Reference architecture: hosted -> dedicated -> private migration path with network diagrams and port/IP lists

8) Short case study (one-page)
- Customer: FintechX (large digital lending platform)
- Problem: Unpredictable text-generation costs and noisy latency at peak times, impacting loan decision latency
- Pilot approach: 6-week dedicated pilot that mirrored peak query distribution; instrumented KV cache and workload-aware batching; ran A/B quality evals against incumbent model endpoint
- Outcomes: Reduced 95th percentile latency from 520ms to 210ms, lowered per-token cost by 28% after batching and quantization, achieved production rollout within 10 weeks.
- Customer quote: \"Redwood cut our vendor drift time and gave us predictable latency at scale—critical for our underwriting SLAs.\" — VP Engineering, FintechX

9) Security FAQ for prospects (short answers for the field)
Q: How is customer data isolated?
A: Hosted customers are segmented by tenant with strict access controls; Dedicated customers receive dedicated GPU pools with network isolation; Private deployments run in customer VPC/on-prem under their network controls.

Q: Can we bring our own KMS/HSM?
A: Yes. Redwood supports KMS integrations (AWS KMS, Google KMS, Azure Key Vault) and HSM-backed key wrapping for Private deployments.

Q: What audit logging is available during a pilot?
A: We provide request-level logs (redacted for PII by default), model invocation traces, and token-level cost breakdowns. Logs retention is configurable per customer and included in the pilot evidence package.

Q: How do you prevent model-regression and data leaks during pilot?
A: We encourage pre-defined eval sets, continuous quality monitoring tied to alerts, and optional streaming redactors or prompt sanitization hooks in the SDK. For sensitive data, use Private deployment and endpoint-level masking.

Q: What compliance standards do you support?
A: Redwood provides controls and artifacts aligned with SOC-like security programs, supports customer-managed private deployments for stricter regimes, and offers data residency controls for EU/UK. Specific audits or evidence packages can be requested during the pilot.

10) Playbook checklist (for AE/SE before pilot close)
- [ ] Pilot charter signed and success criteria scored
- [ ] Baseline performance and cost dashboard shared with customer
- [ ] Security evidence pack attached (KMS guide, audit log sample, retention options)
- [ ] Clear production migration path (timing, cost delta, network changes)
- [ ] Renewal proposal includes: capacity sizing, committed discounts, SLA language, add-on observability retention

Appendix: Assets and templates to attach
- Pitch deck outline (5 slides): problem, architecture, demo plan, pilot metrics, commercial ask
- Pilot measurement template (CSV) with columns: timestamp, endpoint, model_variant, latency_ms, tokens_in, tokens_out, cache_hit, error_code
- Technical annex examples: canary policy YAML snippet, autoscaling thresholds JSON, network egress/IP list

YAML canary policy example (snippet):

---
canary:
  traffic_percent: 10
  duration: 24h
  rollback_condition:
    error_rate_multiplier: 2.0
    min_absolute_error_rate: 0.002
---

How to use this doc:
- Use the playbook as a checklist and attach the technical evidence folder to every pilot proposal. Customize success criteria with the customer's product and finance sponsors and keep a running measurement CSV in the shared pilot workspace.

Owners and contact points:
- GTM playbook owner: Sofia Patel (sofia@redwood.ai)
- Escalation: Solutions Engineering lead, Ethan Morales (ethan@redwood.ai)
- Security liaison: Priya Natarajan (priya@redwood.ai)

title: Technical economics and risk snapshot — Financial prospects
preview:
Executive summary:

This short playbook is designed for sales engineers and account teams engaging financial-services prospects who must evaluate inference economics, operational risk, and security assurances before pilot approval. The goal: give a repeatable, technical narrative and concrete cost/latency scenarios that accelerate pilot-to-contract conversations while surfacing common compliance objections up front.

Use case scope:
- Target customers: retail banks, payments platforms, wealth-management SaaS, and insurance analytics teams.
- Typical workload patterns: high-concurrency micro-requests (fraud/risk scoring), batch enrichment (overnight customer scoring), and conversational assistants (compliance & advisor workflows).

1) Positioning and core messaging (what to say to buyers)

Elevator (30s):
"Redwood makes inference economics predictable for regulated financial teams: fast, auditable model serving whether you run in our hosted cloud, on dedicated capacity, or inside your VPC — with clear cost controls and fallbacks to keep customer-facing flows within SLA during peak events."

Persona-driven messaging:
- CTO / Head of Platform: "Unifies serving across environments; avoids rewriting inference logic when moving from pilot to production. Reserved capacity + rollout controls reduce operational surprises."
- Head of ML / ML Platform: "Automated batching, KV cache reuse, and quantization recommendations lower per-token costs without manual engineering of the runtime."
- CISO / Compliance: "Private deployment options (VPC/on‑prem), KMS/HSM integrations, and per-request logging offer the auditability required for regulated workflows."

Key proof points to cite in meetings:
- Observable per-token breakdowns (tokens, compute, cache hit rate) for each route and model variant.
- Canary/A-B rollout with immediate fallback to smaller model variant if latency SLOs breach.
- Typical cost reductions: 20–45% via intelligent batching and KV caching on classification-heavy workloads (customer examples available).

2) Competitive brief (single-slide summary for buyer decks)

Competitors: In-house self-hosted runtimes, Major cloud vendor managed endpoints, LLM API providers with hosted-only offerings.

How Redwood differentiates (short):
- Unified runtime across hosted, dedicated, and private deployments (vs hosted-only vendors).
- Explicit economic controls (batching policies, per-route quotas, quantization profiles) not typically exposed by hyperscalers.
- Production-focused tools (token-level cost breakdown, rollback policies, per-request traceability) geared to regulated environments.

Comparison table (abbreviated):
| Dimension                 | Redwood                                 | Hyperscaler managed  | Self-hosted open runtime |
|--------------------------|-----------------------------------------|----------------------|--------------------------|
| Deployment models        | Hosted / Dedicated / Private            | Hosted / Dedicated   | Private only             |
| Per-request audit trail  | Yes                                     | Partial              | Varies                   |
| Cost control primitives  | Batching, cache, quant profiles, routing| Limited              | Engineering heavy        |
| SLA + enterprise support | SLA-backed Dedicated + enterprise SLAs  | Varies               | Depends on provider      |

3) Pilot architecture and sample runbook (technical audience)

Recommended pilot topology (3-week pilot):
- Week 0: Agreement + security questionnaire -> define data flows and ingress model.
- Week 1: Onboard to a dedicated sandbox environment (private routing optional). Integrate KMS for key management.
- Week 2: Run representative traffic (synthetic and replay) and collect token/latency/cost traces.
- Week 3: Tune batching/cache/quantization, present results and recommended committed tier.

Architecture (text diagram):
- Client services -> (1) Redwood Edge Gateway (TLS) -> (2) Inference Router -> (3) Model Serving Pool (dedicated GPUs) -> (4) Observability & Audit Log Sink (SIEM/Kafka)

Runbook (high level):
1. Create sandbox org and generate API keys (owner: SE).
2. Provide sample dataset (anonymized) and representative traffic profile (owner: customer eng).
3. Run 48-hour replay at target QPS to validate tail latency and cache hit rates (owner: perf eng).
4. Apply batching window and prefix cache settings; measure cost delta (owner: runtime team).
5. Deliver pilot report with cost/latency/SLO attainment and recommended committed capacity (owner: SE).

4) Concrete pricing guidance & negotiation levers (playbook for AE/SE)

Pricing primitives to present:
- Hosted (pay-as-you-go): token-based usage billing. Example ballpark: $0.004–$0.012 per 1k prompt tokens depending on model class and concurrency.
- Dedicated (reserved): Monthly committed spend, volume-discount tiers, SLA crediting. Typical commitment: 1–3 months minimum for pilot proofs of concept.
- Private (VPC/on-prem): Platform licensing + support fee (annual). Includes private control plane, extended retention for logs, and on-prem packaging.

Negotiation levers to accelerate pilots:
- Pilot credit (one-off $X): covers first N tokens or two-week dedicated capacity.
- Pilot discount: time-boxed discount for first 90 days if committing to reserved capacity within 60 days of pilot close.
- Engineering assurance: guaranteed onboarding window and dedicated SRE hours (billed or included in package depending on tier).

Pricing scenario (example) — fraud scoring workload:
- Workload: 25 requests/s steady, each request 120 prompt tokens + 30 completion tokens, 30% prefix cache hit.
- Effective tokens/s: 25 * (120+30) * (1 - 0.3) = 25 * 150 * 0.7 = 2,625 tokens/s => ~9.45M tokens/hour.
- Hosted cost (example): 9.45M tokens/hr * 24 hr * $0.006 / 1000 = ~$1,361/day.
- With dedicated + batching/cache tuning: expect 30–40% cost reduction.

5) Short case study (anonymized, representative)

Customer: "Global Payments Platform" (anonymized)
Problem: Real-time fraud scoring had unpredictable per-transaction costs and missed SLA targets during peak.
Actions taken:
- Migrated scoring pipelines to dedicated pools with prefix KV cache for merchant context.
- Implemented conservative batching windows for low-latency paths and aggressive batching for backfill jobs.
Results:
- 37% reduction in per-transaction inference cost.
- 95th percentile latency reduced from 220 ms to 120 ms for real-time scoring path.
- Audit logs integrated into SIEM for proof-of-replay in investigations.

6) Security & compliance FAQ (common prospect questions and short answers)

Q: Can Redwood run entirely inside my VPC or on-prem?
A: Yes. We offer VPC deployment and an on-prem package. The private deployment keeps the control plane in your network and integrates with your KMS/HSM for key management.

Q: How long are audit logs retained and can we export them?
A: Logs are retained per the contract; typical pilot retention is 90 days. Export to S3/Kafka or direct SIEM integration is supported and can be included in dedicated/private offers.

Q: Is data used to retrain Redwood models?
A: Customer data is never used to retrain Redwood public models. For hosted services we provide data isolation guarantees and options to opt-out of aggregated telemetry. Private deployments never send customer payloads to Redwood-managed training pipelines.

Q: How do you prove model outputs weren't tampered with?
A: Per-request signed envelopes (HMAC) and immutable audit logs with sequence numbers can be enabled. We also provide deterministic request tracing to map inputs to outputs and system metadata for each inference call.

Q: What are typical SLOs we can expect?
A: Example published SLOs for Dedicated: 99.9% availability, 95th percentile tail latency target of 200ms for short-context classification models; targets vary by model complexity. For regulated customers we negotiate tailored SLOs in the commercial contract.

7) Objection handling cheatsheet

Objection: "We can't accept hosted data flows."
Answer: Present private deployment + KMS/HSM + network peering options. Offer a time-limited on-prem pilot if needed.
Objection: "We need guaranteed costs under heavy load."
Answer: Recommend Dedicated with reserved throughput and pre-defined fallback models. Show pilot runbook demonstrating behavior under replayed peak loads.

8) Suggested customer deliverables (what to hand over post-pilot)
- Pilot findings document: latency & cost baseline, tuning recommendations, recommended committed tier.
- Operational runbook: deployment, incident escalation, rollback steps.
- Security annex: signed architecture diagram, KMS integration summary, log retention & export config.

9) Appendix — example API call and simple cost calculator

Sample cURL (anonymized):
curl -X POST https://api.redwood.example/v1/generate \ 
  -H "Authorization: Bearer <API_KEY>" \ 
  -H "Content-Type: application/json" \ 
  -d '{"model":"rw-medium-2026","prompt":"Assess transaction risk: {payload}","max_tokens":64}'

Quick cost calculator (text):
- tokens_per_request = prompt_tokens + completion_tokens
- effective_tokens = tokens_per_request * requests_per_second * (1 - cache_hit_rate)
- hourly_tokens = effective_tokens * 3600
- cost_per_hour = hourly_tokens / 1000 * unit_price_per_1k_tokens

Contact and ownership:
- Primary SE: Maya Chen (mchen@redwood.example)
- Deal lead template: AE + SE + Solutions Architect + Trust engineer on first 2 weeks of pilot.

Next steps (for AE/SE):
1. Confirm prospect's winning metric (cost reduction, latency, auditability).
2. Provision sandbox org and schedule replay window.
3. Provide pilot commercial terms and lock in pilot credit/engineering hours.

Document history and references:
- Drafted: 2026-04-15 by Maya Chen
- Reviewed by: Luca Romano, Priya Natarajan
- Related artifacts: pilot-template-contract, dedicated-capacity-SLA-terms, private-deploy-checklist

End of document.

title: Heterogeneous Deployment Sales Brief for Technical Buyers
preview:
Summary:
This brief equips sales and solutions engineers with a concise, deployment-focused narrative for technical buyers considering a mixed deployment strategy: combining Redwood Hosted, Redwood Dedicated (reserved capacity), and Redwood Private (VPC/on‑prem). It explains when to recommend each option, how to quantify latency and cost tradeoffs, competitive positioning vs cloud-managed endpoints, and an objection-handling playbook for compliance, reliability, and TCO concerns.

Audience:
- Platform engineers evaluating production constraints
- Security/compliance stakeholders assessing data residency and auditability
- Architects designing multi-region, mixed-capacity inference topologies

1) One-line value proposition for technical buyers:
Redwood enables predictable, low-latency LLM inference at the unit economics and operational maturity required for production — and lets you mix hosted, reserved, and private capacity at route level so your app meets SLAs without rebuilding your stack.

2) When to recommend each deployment mode (quick checklist):
- Hosted API: rapid onboarding, experimental workloads, or low-SLA features where TTV matters. Recommend when: team wants SDK-level speed-to-product (<1 day), no strict data residency, expected steady monthly usage < 200M tokens.
- Dedicated: long-running, high-throughput endpoints with predictable demand and strong isolation. Recommend when: 95th percentile latency targets are strict, predictable sustained traffic > 1M tokens/day, or contract-level SLAs required.
- Private (VPC/on-prem): highly regulated or air-gapped workloads with strict control over keys, audit trails, and network egress. Recommend when: data residency, E2E auditability, or internal governance policies forbid cloud-stored PII.

3) Concrete SLO/operational examples to use in conversations:
- Typical Hosted baseline: median latency for a 512-token generation on a Redwood-curated Llama-variant: 55–80 ms median, 95th percentile 140–220 ms (depending on region & model).
- Dedicated baseline (reserved GPU pool): 95th percentile 60–90 ms for the same workload when steady-state batching and pinned kernels are enabled.
- Private (on-prem with optimized inference stack): achievable 95th percentile 50–80 ms when matching equivalent GPU class and using Redwood’s runtime.
- Availability SLOs: Dedicated customers often purchase 99.9% SLA with automatic failover policies; Hosted is covered by best-effort SLOs and visibility dashboards.

4) Pricing stanzas and example scenarios (talking points; use formal quote for commitments):
- Hosted: list-style per-token pricing (example anchor): $0.012 per 1k prompt tokens + $0.015 per 1k generation tokens for LLM-x family. Example: 1M prompt + 2M gen tokens/month -> ~$45/month.
- Dedicated: base reservation + usage multiplier. Example: 8-GPU reserved cluster $18k/month + usage add-on $0.006 per 1k tokens beyond baseline. For predictable high-volume customers, Dedicated typically reduces per-token unit cost by 40–70% vs Hosted.
- Private: platform licensing + installation and support (one-time setup + annual maintenance). Example: 1x rack on-prem bundle $120k initial + $35k/year support; per-token costs modeled on GPU utilization and ops.
- Hybrid recommendations: show blended TCO for migration phases (pilot on Hosted, steady-state on Dedicated, controlled PII routes on Private). Use a 12-month model: estimate 6 months Hosted pilot then 6 months Dedicated ramp.

5) Competitive brief (neutral language) — common competitors and positioning points:
- Competitor A (large cloud vendor managed endpoints):
  - Strengths: deep cloud integration, single-bill simplicity.
  - Redwood counter: our runtime and routing policies reduce per-token cost under bursty traffic by 20–50% via batching and KV caching; we provide a unified API across deployment modes so migration and governance are simpler.
- Competitor B (self-hosted inference vendors):
  - Strengths: tight on-prem control, appeal to infra teams.
  - Redwood counter: we combine on-prem packages with a managed control plane option, reducing ops burden while preserving audit/logging and KMS integrations.
- Competitor C (LLM API providers focused on simplicity):
  - Strengths: easy developer UX and fast iteration.
  - Redwood counter: we match developer ergonomics while adding route-level policies, cost controls, and performance predictability required for production.

6) Objection handling (scenarios and short scripts):
- "We can just use the cloud provider's model endpoints."
  - Response: "Cloud endpoints are convenient, but for production economics and predictable latency under bursty load, Redwood’s runtime and routing reduce per-token costs and guardrails (batching, prefix cache, kernel tuning) that cloud endpoints do not expose. We’ll run a 2-week pilot and show per-token delta and 95th latency graphs."
- "We need absolute data residency and no control plane exposure."
  - Response: "Redwood Private supports a customer-controlled control plane in a VPC or fully air-gapped on-prem package with KMS/HSM integrations and local audit logging. We will map the data flows, provide architecture diagrams, and run a compliance checklist with your security team."
- "We don’t want vendor lock-in for model formats."
  - Response: "Redwood serves common open checkpoints and supports model version pinning and exports of model artifacts and KV cache snapshots. Migration paths are part of the Dedicated contract and private deployment runbook."

7) Pitch deck outline (single-slide bullets for rapid customization):
- Slide 1: Customer challenge — inconsistent latency, unpredictable token spend, compliance constraints.
- Slide 2: Redwood approach — unified runtime + routing + mixed deployments.
- Slide 3: Economics snapshot — Hosted vs Dedicated vs Private TCO for a sample workload.
- Slide 4: Architecture patterns — pilot, steady-state, PII-only private routes. Diagram: client -> route policy -> hosted/dedicated/private fallback.
- Slide 5: Risk mitigation — canary rollouts, automatic fallback to cheaper variants, audit trails.
- Slide 6: Customer case study (anonymized).
- Slide 7: Ask & next steps — pilot proposal, data-sharing checklist, timelines.

8) Short anonymized case study (1 paragraph & metrics):
- Customer: large B2B SaaS in North America (onboarding by Redwood Solutions Eng).
- Challenge: production chat assistant with 600k requests/day, 95th percentile spikes causing >200 ms latency and rising token bill.
- Solution: mixed deployment — high-frequency stateful routes to Dedicated with prefix caching; analytics and low-volume non-sensitive work on Hosted.
- Outcome after 90 days: 40% reduction in per-token cost, 30% improvement in 95th percentile latency, and stabilized error rate <0.1%.

9) Security & compliance FAQ (short answers for SEs):
- Q: Does Redwood store customer prompts or embeddings in the hosted offering?
  - A: By default, minimal telemetry is stored for billing and analytics. Customers can opt-out of retention or move sensitive routes to Dedicated/Private where logs are retained per customer policy.
- Q: Can we integrate our own KMS/HSM?
  - A: Yes. Dedicated and Private support customer-managed keys; Hosted supports KMS integrations for enterprise contracts.
- Q: How are audit logs exported for SOC2/ISO reviews?
  - A: We provide log export mechanisms (S3, secure syslog) and standardized evidence packages for enterprise audits.
- Q: What network boundaries exist for Private deployments?
  - A: Private can be deployed fully inside a customer VPC or on-prem with no egress to Redwood-hosted control planes if required.

10) Pilot playbook (3-step commercial + technical skeleton):
1. Discovery (1 week): collect traffic patterns, PII mapping, and SLO targets.
2. Pilot (2–6 weeks): deploy targeted routes to Hosted and Dedicated; measure latency, token usage, and failover. Provide weekly dashboards and a final TCO runbook.
3. Scale & contract (4–8 weeks): finalize reservations, security attestation, and production cutover plan.

11) Assets and attachments to include with outreach:
- Sample pilot statement of work (SOW) and pricing worksheet (xls).
- Architecture diagrams (Hosted/Dedicated/Private hybrid).
- Benchmark reports: 95th-latency dashboards, per-token cost breakdowns for sample LLM families.
- Compliance checklist and KB for Private installs.

12) Owner & escalation path:
- Primary owner for this brief: Ava Chen (Solutions Engineering).
- Technical reviewer: Miguel Ortega (Platform Eng).
- Security reviewer: Priya Raman (Security).
- Escalation: sols-eng@redwood.ai -> go-to-market-leads@redwood.ai -> eng-platform-oncall@redwood.ai

13) Recommended next steps for SEs engaging prospects:
- Run the 10-minute economics check: estimate monthly tokens, identify PII surfaces, recommend pilot mode (Hosted pilot vs dedicated pilot).
- Book architecture workshop within 72 hours of signaled interest.
- Prepare the hybrid TCO model and a 2-week performance validation plan.

Document history and references:
- Created: 2026-02-15
- Last updated: 2026-03-20
- Related internal references: eng-private-deployments/deployment-guides, eng-serving-runtime/runtime-architecture, sales-enablement/pilot-activation-playbook-and-technical-economics-2027

Feedback and updates: please send suggested edits and data points to Ava Chen.

  - Strengths: easy developer UX and fast iteration.
  - Redwood counter: we match developer ergonomics while adding route-level policies, cost controls, and performance predictability required for production.

6) Objection handling (scenarios and short scripts):
- "We can just use the cloud provider's model endpoints."
  - Response: "Cloud endpoints are convenient, but for production economics and predictable latency under bursty load, Redwood’s runtime and routing reduce per-token costs and guardrails (batching, prefix cache, kernel tuning) that cloud endpoints do not expose. We’ll run a 2-week pilot and show per-token delta and 95th latency graphs."
- "We need absolute data residency and no control plane exposure."
  - Response: "Redwood Private supports a customer-controlled control plane in a VPC or fully air-gapped on-prem package with KMS/HSM integrations and local audit logging. We will map the data flows, provide architecture diagrams, and run a compliance checklist with your security team."
- "We don’t want vendor lock-in for model formats."
  - Response: "Redwood serves common open checkpoints and supports model version pinning and exports of model artifacts and KV cache snapshots. Migration paths are part of the Dedicated contract and private deployment runbook."

7) Pitch deck outline (single-slide bullets for rapid customization):
- Slide 1: Customer challenge — inconsistent latency, unpredictable token spend, compliance constraints.
- Slide 2: Redwood approach — unified runtime + routing + mixed deployments.
- Slide 3: Economics snapshot — Hosted vs Dedicated vs Private TCO for a sample workload.
- Slide 4: Architecture patterns — pilot, steady-state, PII-only private routes. Diagram: client -> route policy -> hosted/dedicated/private fallback.
- Slide 5: Risk mitigation — canary rollouts, automatic fallback to cheaper variants, audit trails.
- Slide 6: Customer case study (anonymized).
- Slide 7: Ask & next steps — pilot proposal, data-sharing checklist, timelines.

8) Short anonymized case study (1 paragraph & metrics):
- Customer: large B2B SaaS in North America (onboarding by Redwood Solutions Eng).
- Challenge: production chat assistant with 600k requests/day, 95th percentile spikes causing >200 ms latency and rising token bill.
- Solution: mixed deployment — high-frequency stateful routes to Dedicated with prefix caching; analytics and low-volume non-sensitive work on Hosted.
- Outcome after 90 days: 40% reduction in per-token cost, 30% improvement in 95th percentile latency, and stabilized error rate <0.1%.

9) Security & compliance FAQ (short answers for SEs):
- Q: Does Redwood store customer prompts or embeddings in the hosted offering?
  - A: By default, minimal telemetry is stored for billing and analytics. Customers can opt-out of retention or move sensitive routes to Dedicated/Private where logs are retained per customer policy.
- Q: Can we integrate our own KMS/HSM?
  - A: Yes. Dedicated and Private support customer-managed keys; Hosted supports KMS integrations for enterprise contracts.
- Q: How are audit logs exported for SOC2/ISO reviews?
  - A: We provide log export mechanisms (S3, secure syslog) and standardized evidence packages for enterprise audits.
- Q: What network boundaries exist for Private deployments?
  - A: Private can be deployed fully inside a customer VPC or on-prem with no egress to Redwood-hosted control planes if required.

10) Pilot playbook (3-step commercial + technical skeleton):
1. Discovery (1 week): collect traffic patterns, PII mapping, and SLO targets.
2. Pilot (2–6 weeks): deploy targeted routes to Hosted and Dedicated; measure latency, token usage, and failover. Provide weekly dashboards and a final TCO runbook.
3. Scale & contract (4–8 weeks): finalize reservations, security attestation, and production cutover plan.

11) Assets and attachments to include with outreach:
- Sample pilot statement of work (SOW) and pricing worksheet (xls).
- Architecture diagrams (Hosted/Dedicated/Private hybrid).
- Benchmark reports: 95th-latency dashboards, per-token cost breakdowns for sample LLM families.
- Compliance checklist and KB for Private installs.

12) Owner & escalation path:
- Primary owner for this brief: Ava Chen (Solutions Engineering).
- Technical reviewer: Miguel Ortega (Platform Eng).
- Security reviewer: Priya Raman (Security).
- Escalation: sols-eng@redwood.ai -> go-to-market-leads@redwood.ai -> eng-platform-oncall@redwood.ai

13) Recommended next steps for SEs engaging prospects:
- Run the 10-minute economics check: estimate monthly tokens, identify PII surfaces, recommend pilot mode (Hosted pilot vs dedicated pilot).
- Book architecture workshop within 72 hours of signaled interest.
- Prepare the hybrid TCO model and a 2-week performance validation plan.

- Created: 2026-02-15
- Last updated: 2026-03-20
- Related internal references: eng-private-deployments/deployment-guides, eng-serving-runtime/runtime-architecture, sales-enablement/pilot-activation-playbook-and-technical-economics-2027

Feedback and updates: please send suggested edits and data points to Ava Chen.

title: Retail & Commerce Inference Sales Kit — Positioning, Pitch, Pricing, and Security FAQ
preview:
Overview

This sales kit is designed for sellers and solutions engineers working outbound or responding to RFPs in Retail & Commerce. It combines crisp positioning, a competitive brief, a pitch-deck outline, price/TCO guidance, a one-page case study, a demo recipe, and a short Security FAQ tailored for procurement and technical evaluation.

Audience and target personas

- Head of Product / Director of Search: cares about relevance, conversion lift, latency and cost per query.
- Engineering Manager (ML/Infra): needs predictable inference runtimes, versioned rollouts, and observability.
- Platform/Cloud Architect: evaluates network, VPC, and deployment models (hosted vs dedicated vs private).
- Procurement / Legal: looks for pricing predictability, SLAs, and security/compliance evidence.

Key positioning and messaging (Retail-focused)

Elevator pitch (15s): Redwood Inference gives retail teams enterprise-grade LLM serving that improves search relevance and personalization while keeping latency low and operating costs predictable — across hosted, reserved, or private deployments.

Top line value props (for pitch & collateral):
- Conversion-first inference: optimized runtimes and caching reduce median query latency to 50–120ms at scale, improving add-to-cart and checkout conversion.
- Cost predictability: reserved/Dedicated GPU pools and unit economics tooling reduce per-query cost up to 6x vs naïve cloud-hosted endpoints for high QPS workloads.
- Safe rollouts and regressions guardrails: canary rollouts, eval integration, and quality monitoring prevent relevance regressions in production.
- Hybrid deployment flexibility: self-hosted/private options for PII-sensitive catalogs and hosted API for rapid POC.

Use cases and value metrics (quick bullets for discovery)

1) Query rewriting & relevance tuning
- Outcome: 8–20% improvement in click-through and add-to-cart for long-tail queries.
- Metrics to ask for: daily search QPS, current median latency, % long-tail queries, baseline conversion rate.

2) Conversational shopping / chat assistants
- Outcome: reduced contact center volume, increased AOV via guided upsell flows.
- Ask for: monthly conversations, average session turns, SLA for response latency.

3) Product summarization & auto-descriptions (catalog enrichment)
- Outcome: faster catalog onboarding, consistent description quality, better SEO.
- Ask for: catalog size, ingestion frequency, human review ratio.

4) Personalization & ranking re-rank
- Outcome: lift in basket size and time-on-site; reranking latency budgets.
- Ask for: rerank QPS, batch vs online constraints, click-through baselines.

Competitive brief (battlecard)

- Major cloud LLM endpoints (AWS/Azure/GCP managed LLM services):
  - Strengths: easy integration, broad platform services, enterprise contracts.
  - Weaknesses / friction: limited fine-grained performance tuning for inference, less control over GPU stack, and fewer integrated caching/batching optimizations that matter at high QPS.
  - Redwood counter: Our runtime selects kernels, manages KV cache and continuous batching automatically and exposes predictable unit economics for production retail workloads.

- Open-source self-hosted stacks + internal infra teams:
  - Strengths: full control, potential low model licensing costs.
  - Weaknesses: high operational burden, variance in latency, ongoing engineering cost for GPU ops and rollout tooling.
  - Redwood counter: Offers the same deployment flexibility with turnkey tooling (rollouts, metrics, SLOs) and a productized private option to reduce ops lift.

- Specialized inference vendors (inference runtimes / orchestration startups):
  - Strengths: high-performance primitives, tight optimization claims.
  - Weaknesses: narrow scope; may lack product integrations (console, evals, pricing tooling, enterprise SLAs).
  - Redwood counter: Combines a production-first platform (console, observability, rollouts) with kernel and runtime optimizations—bridging engineering and product needs.

Win themes to emphasize during discovery

- Measureable conversion impact (show a short case-driven ROI)
- Predictable unit economics at scale (dedicated capacity + Optimize recommendations)
- Low-friction deployment path: POC on hosted API -> reserved Dedicated pool -> private deployment if needed
- Enterprise controls: VPC, KMS integration, audit logs, role-based access

Pricing guidance and sample TCOs (retail scenarios)

High-level guidance: Redwood offers three pricing levers: hosted-per-use (best for POC & low-volume), Dedicated reserved capacity (best for steady high-QPS workloads), and Private (enterprise licensing for regulated deployments). Use these worked examples in commercial conversations.

Scenario A: Mid-market retailer running search + personalization (steady 200 QPS, ~30M tokens/day)
- Recommended plan: Dedicated reserved capacity (monthly commit) with 2x burst capacity
- Expected benefits: 3–5x cost reduction vs unmanaged hosted endpoints at this scale; stable latency SLOs for search.

Scenario B: Enterprise omnichannel retailer (peak 2000 QPS during promotions, heavy rerank workloads)
- Recommended plan: Hybrid approach — hosted API for low-priority workloads, Dedicated pools for search/rerank, optional Private for PII-sensitive catalog processing.
- Commercial terms to pursue: 6–12 month commit with burst engineering support, SLA add-on for promotional periods.

Pricing examples (do not treat as final — use for guidance in deals):

- Hosted: $0.0008–$0.003 per token-equivalent depending on model class and throughput.
- Dedicated base: monthly capacity payment + reduced per-token compute for steady-state throughput. Typical committed monthly price starts in low five-figures for a production small retailer up to mid-six-figures for enterprise.

Use these numbers only as directional; always route to pricing@redwood or solutions-engineering for formal quotes.

Pitch deck outline (10 slides recommended)

1. Title + one-line value prop
2. Retail challenges: conversion, latency, cost, quality regressions
3. Redwood solution overview (hosted/dedicated/private)
4. How it works: runtime + caching + routing + rollouts (1-slide architecture)
5. Case study / customer outcomes (numbers)
6. Demo: search rerank + conversational quick flow (link to demo recipe)
7. Pricing approach & deployment path
8. Security & compliance (VPC, KMS, audit logs)
9. Implementation plan & timeline (POC -> pilot -> production)
10. Ask and next steps

Demo recipe (solutions engineer playbook)

Objective: Show a live search rerank POC that reduces latency and increases relevance for business-critical queries in 45 minutes.

Prep steps (30–60 minutes prior):
1. Ask customer to provide a small catalog CSV (<= 2k SKUs) and a sample of 200 real search queries with click labels.
2. Preload catalog into a demo index and enable a small hosted model instance.
3. Prepare two routes: baseline (current ranking) and Redwood model + rerank.

During demo (20–25 minutes):
1. Quick context (2m): what we measure and success metric (e.g., position-weighted CTR).
2. Live queries (10m): run sample queries side-by-side and show latency, token usage and result changes.
3. Observability (5m): show per-route token cost, latency percentiles and error rates in Console.
4. Next steps (3m): touch on Dedicated option and timelines.

Objection handling (short scripts)

- "We can self-host and save money."
  - Reply: Self-hosting can save model licensing but operational and engineering costs for GPU ops, kernel tuning, and building rollouts usually exceed savings for production-grade throughput. We offer private deployments and transfer operational knowledge during onboarding.

- "Latency during peak sales is non-negotiable."
  - Reply: We support SLO-driven routing and Dedicated capacity with burst policies; for major promotional events we can pre-stage capacity and provide SLA add-ons.

Customer case snapshot (one-pager you can hand to prospects)

Title: "How Northside Apparel cut search latency by 55% and lifted add-to-cart by 12%"
- Customer: Northside Apparel (hypothetical profile: omnichannel retailer, 12M annual sessions)
- Problem: outdated search infra with high tail latency and poor mobile conversion
- Solution: POC using Redwood hosted API for rerank + Dedicated pool for production; KV prefix caching for common queries and a canary rollout.
- Outcomes (90-day): median search latency from 410ms -> 180ms, add-to-cart +12%, cost per search down 35% vs previous managed endpoint.

Security FAQ for prospect evaluations (concise answers)

Q: Can Redwood run inside our VPC / on-prem?
A: Yes. Redwood Private provides a customer-managed control plane with optional on-prem packaging for air-gapped environments. Many enterprise retail customers run catalog ingestion and PII-sensitive transforms in private deployments.

Q: What encryption & key management options exist?
A: All data at rest and in transit is encrypted. We integrate with customer KMS/HSM for private deployments and support customer-managed keys for Dedicated and Private offerings.

Q: How do you handle access controls and auditing?
A: Role-based access control (RBAC) at the Console level, API keys with scoped privileges, and audit logs that capture request metadata, model versions, and operator actions — exportable to SIEM.

Q: Do you persist user prompts or customer PII?
A: For hosted API, data persistence policies are configurable per contract; we provide options for non-retention for PII and can route sensitive processing to Private deployments. See evidence pack for specific retention policies.

Q: What certifications and compliance evidence do you provide?
A: We publish SOC2 Type II and maintain a compliance evidence portal for enterprise procurement. For sector-specific requirements we work with customers on addendum contracts and data residency commitments.

Q: Can Redwood prove no model regression after updates?
A: Yes. Our rollout system integrates with evals and regression monitoring; canary rollouts and automatic rollback rules are available to prevent quality regressions.

Sales motion and recommended next steps (seller checklist)

1. Discovery: collect QPS, latency targets, catalog size, and sample queries.
2. Business case framing: show conversion-impact scenarios and TCO examples tailored to their metrics.
3. POC offer: 2–4 week hosted POC with provided sample prompt sets and eval harness.
4. Pilot: Dedicated reserved pool for production pilot with SLA and support.
5. Enterprise conversion: negotiate private deployment or 12-month dedicated commit, finalize security addenda.

Appendix and links (internal)

- Demo assets: /confluence/eng-developer-experience/quickstarts-and-templates/retail-demo-assets
- Pricing ops: pricing@redwood.internal
- Security evidence portal: /confluence/security-and-compliance/compliance-evidence

Contact

- Solutions lead: Priya Natarajan (priya@redwood.internal)
- SE on-call for demos: se-oncall@redwood.internal

Last updated: 2026-03-01 (controls and pricing guidance reviewed with finance and product)
