## Purpose
This page is the canonical “guardrails” reference for Security FAQ and RFP responses. It exists to prevent over-commitment, inconsistent wording, and accidental promises (especially around compliance certifications, data residency, retention, and testing).

**Use this page when:**
- You are answering a security questionnaire or RFP.
- A prospect asks for a “simple yes/no” on a nuanced topic.
- You need approved phrasing that is accurate across **Hosted**, **Dedicated**, and **Private** deployments.

**Do not improvise** security/legal claims. If a question goes beyond the approved language below, use the escalation guidance at the end.

---

## Golden rules (read first)
1) **Describe what exists today; never promise roadmap.** If the customer asks “when,” reply with: “We can’t make commitments outside our product and contract. We can share current capabilities and discuss requirements with our team.”
2) **Be deployment-mode specific.** Many controls differ between Hosted vs Dedicated vs Private (VPC/on-prem). If you’re unsure which mode applies, ask.
3) **Avoid absolute statements** unless Legal/Security has approved them in the RFP library (e.g., “never,” “always,” “guarantee,” “fully compliant”).
4) **Evidence is gated.** SOC 2 reports, detailed pen test letters, and sensitive architecture details are shared via the evidence request process (typically under NDA).
5) **If the questionnaire is trying to force a “yes,” provide an accurate “alternate control” statement** and reference the exception policy.

---

## Approved language patterns (copy/paste)
### 1) “Can you share your SOC 2 report?”
**Do say:**
- “Redwood Inference maintains a SOC 2 Type II report. We can share the report under NDA via our evidence request process.”

**Don’t say:**
- “We can send it right now.” (Don’t bypass NDA / process.)
- “We are SOC 2 certified.” (SOC 2 is an attestation/report, not a certification.)

### 2) “Do you train models on customer data?”
**Do say (standard):**
- “By default, Redwood does not use customer prompts, completions, files, or embeddings to train or fine-tune foundation models.”
- “Customer content is processed to provide the service and may be handled by authorized personnel only as needed for security, abuse prevention, support, and reliability, subject to access controls and contractual commitments.”

**Don’t say:**
- “We never access customer data.” (Support/security access may occur under controls.)
- “We don’t store any data.” (Retention varies by configuration and telemetry/logging.)

### 3) “Are you ISO 27001 certified?”
**Do say:**
- “Redwood is not currently ISO/IEC 27001 certified. We align our security program to widely adopted security best practices and have controls that map to ISO/IEC 27001 Annex A domains. We can discuss our control set and provide SOC 2 evidence under NDA.”

**Don’t say:**
- “We are ISO compliant.”
- “We meet ISO 27001 requirements.” (Unless certified; avoid implying certification.)

---

## Topic-by-topic guidance (Do / Don’t / Notes)

### A) SOC 2 (Type II)
**Do say:**
- “Redwood maintains a SOC 2 Type II report covering relevant Trust Services Criteria (Security; additional criteria as scoped in the report).”
- “SOC 2 evidence is available under NDA; request via the SOC 2 evidence playbook.”

**Don’t say:**
- “SOC 2 certified.”
- “Covers all products and all regions.” (Scope is defined in the report; do not generalize.)

**Notes:**
- If the customer requests control-by-control mappings, route to GRC. Provide only approved mapping excerpts from the RFP library/claims matrix.

---

### B) ISO/IEC 27001 (alignment vs certification)
**Do say:**
- “Not currently ISO 27001 certified; we can share how our controls map to key ISO domains (access control, cryptography, operations security, supplier relationships, incident management).”

**Don’t say:**
- “ISO compliant / certified / audited.”
- “In progress to certify by <date>.” (No timelines; no roadmap commitments.)

**Notes:**
- If a questionnaire requires a “Yes” for ISO certification, use the exception policy: provide an alternate statement + SOC 2 evidence path.

---

### C) Data residency (where data is processed/stored)
**Do say:**
- “Data residency depends on the selected deployment mode and region. We support region selection for applicable workloads and can discuss options for EU/US constraints.”
- “Private deployments (VPC/on-prem) provide the strongest residency control because the customer controls the network boundary and hosting environment (subject to the chosen architecture).”

**Don’t say:**
- “We guarantee all data stays in-country.” (Too absolute; depends on region, telemetry, and integrations.)
- “No data ever leaves the EU.” (Avoid absolutes; confirm actual data flows.)

**Notes / nuance to preserve:**
- Clarify *what* data the question refers to: customer content vs metadata/telemetry vs billing/support artifacts.
- If the prospect requests “EU-only support access,” escalate to Security/GRC and Legal (access controls + contractual terms).

---

### D) Data retention & deletion
**Do say:**
- “Retention depends on deployment mode and configuration. We provide documented retention and deletion behavior and, for enterprise plans, configurable retention options for certain logs.”
- “Upon contract termination or upon request (subject to contractual terms), Redwood supports deletion workflows and provides confirmation according to policy.”

**Don’t say:**
- “We retain nothing / zero retention.”
- “We delete immediately everywhere.” (Deletion can be asynchronous across systems/backups; keep wording accurate.)

**Notes:**
- Always reference the customer-facing retention overview for the latest approved specifics.
- If asked about backup retention and purge timelines, do not invent numbers. Escalate to GRC.

---

### E) Encryption (in transit / at rest) and keys (KMS/HSM)
**Do say:**
- “Redwood encrypts data in transit using TLS and encrypts data at rest using industry-standard mechanisms.”
- “Key management approach depends on deployment mode; enterprise customers may have options for customer-managed keys (CMK) or KMS integrations in certain deployments.”

**Don’t say:**
- “We support HSM everywhere.”
- “Customers always hold the keys.” (Shared responsibility varies; only claim where explicitly supported/contracted.)

**Notes:**
- If the customer asks for “BYOK/HYOK” specifics, route to Solutions Engineering + Security for the correct deployment-mode answer.

---

### F) Penetration testing & vulnerability management
**Do say:**
- “Redwood conducts regular security testing, including vulnerability scanning and penetration testing, and remediates findings according to risk-based SLAs.”
- “Pen test summaries/attestations can be shared under NDA where available.”

**Don’t say:**
- “Pen tests happen quarterly.” (Don’t state frequency unless approved and current.)
- “We can share the full pen test report.” (Often restricted; share via evidence process if approved.)

**Notes:**
- If a questionnaire asks for specific scanning tools or exact remediation timelines, use approved ranges from the RFP library or escalate.

---

### G) “No customer data used for training” (definitions and traps)
**Use these definitions to avoid accidental misrepresentation:**
- **Customer content**: prompts, completions, uploaded files/attachments, embeddings, and customer-provided evaluation datasets.
- **Service data/telemetry**: metrics, logs, traces, and operational metadata (may include identifiers; avoid claiming it never exists).

**Do say:**
- “By default, Redwood does not use customer content to train or fine-tune foundation models.”
- “We may process customer content to provide the service and to maintain safety, reliability, and security, under access controls.”

**Don’t say:**
- “We don’t store prompts.” (Not universally true across modes/configs; refer to retention doc.)
- “We never look at prompts.” (Support/security exceptions may apply.)

**If asked about ‘model improvement’:**
- Approved response: “We may use aggregated and de-identified service metrics to improve performance and reliability. We do not use customer content to train foundation models by default.”

---

### H) Subprocessors / vendors
**Do say:**
- “Redwood maintains a subprocessor list and will provide it through the standard procurement path.”
- “Subprocessors are assessed as part of our vendor risk management program.”

**Don’t say:**
- “We have no subprocessors.”
- “We can add your required vendor terms automatically.” (Legal review required.)

**Notes:**
- Any request for special subprocessor notice periods or custom audit rights should go to Legal (Finance & Legal process).

---

### I) HIPAA / BAA / regulated workloads
**Do say:**
- “Redwood supports regulated workloads through deployment and contractual controls; eligibility depends on the deployment model, scope, and the specific regulatory requirements.”
- “We can discuss requirements (including a BAA request) with our Security and Legal teams.”

**Don’t say:**
- “HIPAA compliant out of the box.”
- “We will sign a BAA for any product/use case.”

**Notes:**
- Treat BAA language as Legal-owned. Do not commit on calls or in writing without Legal approval.

---

### J) “Air-gapped” / on-prem claims
**Do say:**
- “Redwood Private includes private deployment options; some customers deploy in isolated environments depending on architecture and requirements.”

**Don’t say:**
- “We support fully air-gapped deployments as a standard offering.” (Only state if explicitly validated for the specific deal.)
- “No external connectivity is required.” (Often untrue for updates, licensing, monitoring, support workflows.)

**Notes:**
- If a customer requires true air-gap, immediately escalate to Solutions Engineering + Product Security PM.

---

### K) Audit logs, SIEM export, and retention
**Do say:**
- “Redwood provides audit logs for key administrative and access events. Export options (e.g., to customer SIEM/storage) depend on deployment and configuration.”
- “Retention options are available on enterprise plans; details are documented in the audit logging overview.”

**Don’t say:**
- “We log everything.”
- “All events are available via API in real time.” (Only claim features that exist.)

**Notes:**
- If a customer provides a required event list, validate against the audit log taxonomy and escalate gaps.

---

## Words/phrases to avoid (unless explicitly approved for that deal)
- “Certified” (unless referring to an actual current certification and within scope)
- “Compliant” (as a blanket statement)
- “Guarantee” / “always” / “never”
- “Meets all requirements of <framework>”
- “Air-gapped” / “zero trust” as marketing shorthand without specifics
- “No data stored” / “no logs” / “no access ever”

Use: “supports,” “provides,” “can be configured,” “subject to contract,” “within scope,” “depending on deployment mode,” “under NDA.”

---

## Escalation: when to stop and ask for review
Escalate to the listed owner(s) if any of the following occur:
- The customer requests **SOC 2 report**, pen test letters, or other restricted evidence → **GRC (Olga Petrov / Daniel Carter)** via evidence request playbook.
- The customer asks for **contractual security commitments** (BAA, breach notification timelines, audit rights, indemnities) → **Legal (Michael Grant) + GRC**.
- The customer requires **country-specific residency guarantees** or “EU-only admin access” → **GRC + Security** (and EMEA reviewer for wording).
- The questionnaire forces a “Yes” that is not accurate → use the **questionnaire exceptions policy** and route to **GRC**.
- The question touches **roadmap / not-yet-shipped features** → **Product Security PM (Naomi Feldman)**.

**Fast path for Sales/SE:** If the deal is time-sensitive, tag the Security review escalation page owners and include (a) exact question, (b) customer-required wording, (c) deployment mode, (d) due date/time zone.

---

## Versioning and citation guidance
- In RFP responses, cite the specific artifact version/date when referencing a Redwood security statement (example: “Redwood Enterprise Security FAQ (Last reviewed: 2026-02-10)”).
- Do not quote Slack messages or “tribal knowledge.” Use the Security FAQ, one-pagers, or RFP library as the source of truth.
- If you must deviate from standard language, follow the RFP approvals SOP and capture the deviation so it can be upstreamed to the claims matrix.

---

## Change log (summary)
**2026-02-14:** Refreshed ISO language to avoid implied certification; tightened data residency “no data leaves region” anti-claim; added standard phrasing for “no training on customer data” with support/security access nuance; aligned pen test wording to avoid fixed frequency claims.

---

## Owners
- **Primary owner:** Product Marketing (Camila Reyes)
- **Security accuracy:** Security Engineering (Marcus Lin, Kevin Osei)
- **Compliance language & evidence:** GRC (Olga Petrov, Daniel Carter)
- **Contractual language:** Legal (Michael Grant)

If you see language in the field that conflicts with this page, file a request against the RFP library process and link the customer prompt/questionnaire item.

title: Security FAQ Change Log & Versioning Policy (2025–2026)
preview:
## Purpose
This page documents (1) the change log for Redwood Inference’s Enterprise Security FAQ and (2) the versioning policy that Sales, Solutions Engineering, Security/GRC, and Customer Success must follow when sharing or citing the FAQ in RFPs and security questionnaires.

This is a “single source of truth” for: what changed, why it changed, who approved it, and how to reference the correct version externally.

---

## Canonical location + what counts as “the FAQ”
**Canonical document (internal):** Confluence page “Redwood Enterprise Security FAQ (Hosted/Dedicated/Private)”.

**Approved external sharing:**
- Sales/SE may share the customer-facing PDF export or customer-safe link *only if* it matches a published version listed below.
- If a prospect requests a SOC 2 report or restricted evidence, do **not** attach it to the FAQ. Use the evidence request process (see related pages).

**Do not** copy/paste older FAQ text from emails, Slack, or Google Drive drafts into an RFP. Use the published version or the approved snippet library.

---

## Versioning policy
### Version format
We use a time-based format:
- **vYYYY.MM** (example: **v2026.02**)

Each published FAQ version has:
- **Version:** vYYYY.MM
- **Published date**
- **Last reviewed date**
- **Approvers** (Security, GRC, Product Security PM; Legal when contractual/commitment wording changes)

### What triggers a new version
We publish a new FAQ version when any of the following changes occur:
1) **Customer-facing claims** change (e.g., retention wording, encryption scope, audit logging/export capabilities).
2) **Control or compliance posture descriptions** change (e.g., SOC 2 period wording, ISO alignment phrasing).
3) **Deployment-mode boundaries** change (Hosted vs Dedicated vs Private shared responsibility).
4) **Process changes** that affect customers (e.g., how to request evidence, SLA/turnaround expectations for evidence requests).

### Major vs minor updates
We do not use semantic “major/minor” numbering; we use a monthly version. However, we classify changes internally to decide communications:

**Material change (requires proactive re-send to in-flight deals):**
- Alters an externally relevant claim or limitation.
- Corrects an ambiguity that could change a customer’s risk assessment.

**Non-material change (no proactive re-send required):**
- Formatting, broken links, clarified internal routing, or non-substantive edits.

The change log below notes whether each release is **Material** or **Non-material**.

### Required approvals
Before publishing any new FAQ version:
- **Security Engineering reviewer:** Marcus Lin (backup: Kevin Osei)
- **GRC reviewer:** Olga Petrov (backup: Daniel Carter)
- **Product Security PM:** Naomi Feldman
- **Legal (conditional):** Michael Grant (required when language could be interpreted as contractual commitments, breach notification terms, indemnities, SLAs, or certifications)
- **GTM enablement owner:** Talia Benson (publication + comms)

### Sales/SE instructions: how to cite the FAQ in RFPs
When referencing the FAQ, include **both**:
1) The version (vYYYY.MM)
2) The published date

**Approved citation line (copy/paste):**
“Reference: Redwood Inference Enterprise Security FAQ **v2026.02** (published **2026-02-12**).”

**If the customer asks ‘is this current?’**
- Respond with the citation line above.
- If the deal spans multiple weeks, confirm the version again at final submission.

**If an RFP asks for ‘policy date’ or ‘last updated’**
- Use the **published date** for that version.
- Do not claim “real-time updated” or “continuously updated.”

**If an RFP asks for ISO 27001 certification**
- Do **not** state or imply certification unless explicitly confirmed by GRC.
- Use the approved phrasing in the FAQ / claims guidance (see related pages).

---

## Publication + deprecation rules
### Publishing
A version is considered published when:
- The Confluence page is updated with the new version header, and
- This change log is updated, and
- Sales enablement index links are updated to the new version.

### Deprecation
- The **current** version is the latest listed as “Published” below.
- Previous versions become **Deprecated** 30 days after a new version is published, unless an enterprise customer requires a frozen packet for procurement. If a customer requires a frozen packet, Sales/SE must:
  - Store the packet in the deal workspace, and
  - Cite the frozen packet version explicitly, and
  - Avoid mixing text from different versions.

---

## Change log (2025–2026)
> Notes:
> - “Why” is written to help Sales/SE understand intent and avoid reintroducing old wording in custom answers.
> - Approvers listed are those who signed off on the *customer-facing language*.

### v2026.02 (Published 2026-02-12) — **Material**
**What changed**
- Clarified **data retention & deletion** language by deployment mode (Hosted vs Dedicated vs Private), separating:
  - request payload handling,
  - system/application logs,
  - audit logs,
  - backup/DR considerations.
- Tightened wording for **EU data residency** claims to avoid implying universal residency for all metadata/logs in all configurations.
- Updated **audit logging** section to reflect current export destinations and common integration patterns (including customer-managed storage / SIEM pipelines) without promising roadmap items.
- Added a “Definitions” block for **“no training on customer data”** to remove ambiguity between (a) model training, (b) transient processing for inference, and (c) limited operational troubleshooting access.

**Why**
- Recurring customer questions in EMEA and regulated industries required more precise boundaries and fewer implied guarantees.
- Reduced back-and-forth during RFPs by pre-answering the most common follow-ups (especially around logs vs prompts and deletion timelines).

**Approvers**
- Dr. Aisha Rahman (CISO)
- Marcus Lin (Security Engineering)
- Olga Petrov (GRC)
- Naomi Feldman (Product Security PM)

---

### v2026.01 (Published 2026-01-17) — **Non-material**
**What changed**
- Reorganized SSO/RBAC content to align with the standard answers page; added cross-links.
- Fixed terminology inconsistencies (e.g., “SSO/SAML” vs “SAML SSO”, “SCIM provisioning”).
- Updated the “How to request SOC 2 report/evidence” section to reference the current intake + NDA steps.

**Why**
- Improve navigability and ensure consistent answers across FAQ and snippet library.

**Approvers**
- Marcus Lin
- Olga Petrov
- Naomi Feldman

---

### v2025.11 (Published 2025-11-21) — **Material**
**What changed**
- Updated **SOC 2** language to use the approved “report available under NDA” wording and removed informal phrasing that could be interpreted as publicly shareable.
- Added explicit statement that security evidence requests are handled via the GRC triage queue with standard timelines.
- Clarified **subprocessor** disclosure approach (where to request the current list) without embedding a static list in the FAQ.

**Why**
- Prevent uncontrolled redistribution of restricted audit artifacts.
- Reduce procurement friction by setting correct expectations and a consistent process.

**Approvers**
- Dr. Aisha Rahman
- Olga Petrov
- Daniel Carter
- Michael Grant

---

### v2025.09 (Published 2025-09-09) — **Material**
**What changed**
- Added a dedicated section on **Private deployment shared responsibility**, including:
  - customer-managed networking boundaries,
  - key management ownership options,
  - audit log responsibilities,
  - support model distinctions from Hosted.
- Refined encryption statements to separate **in-transit**, **at-rest**, and **key management** responsibilities by deployment mode.

**Why**
- Private/VPC deals required consistent, repeatable language that doesn’t over-commit to controls outside Redwood’s control in customer environments.

**Approvers**
- Marcus Lin
- Kevin Osei
- Naomi Feldman
- Michael Grant

---

### v2025.06 (Published 2025-06-03) — **Material**
**What changed**
- Established the FAQ as a versioned artifact and added:
  - version header,
  - owner + review cadence,
  - standardized section order.
- Introduced baseline answers for:
  - vulnerability management,
  - incident response and notification (high-level, non-contractual),
  - access control overview (SSO/RBAC).

**Why**
- Enterprise RFP volume increased; inconsistent ad-hoc answers created rework and risk.

**Approvers**
- Dr. Aisha Rahman
- Olga Petrov
- Naomi Feldman

---

## Review cadence
- **Quarterly**: scheduled review of the FAQ (even if no changes are expected) by Security + GRC + Product Security PM.
- **Out-of-cycle**: allowed when (a) customer feedback indicates ambiguity, (b) product behavior changes, or (c) compliance posture wording must be updated.

---

## How to request a change (internal)
1) File a request in the enablement intake channel or the designated ticketing workflow (link from the enablement kit index).
2) Include:
   - the exact question and customer context,
   - the proposed wording (if any),
   - the deal urgency/date needed,
   - whether the change affects Hosted/Dedicated/Private.
3) Talia Benson coordinates drafting; reviewers sign off per “Required approvals.”
4) Once published, Sales Enablement posts the new version and updates the enablement kit index.

---

## Quick “do / don’t” reminders for Sales and SE
**Do**
- Cite the version and published date in RFP submissions.
- Use approved snippets for high-frequency controls (SSO/RBAC/audit logs).
- Escalate exceptions using the questionnaire exceptions policy.

**Don’t**
- Promise certifications (e.g., ISO 27001) or attestations not explicitly stated in the current FAQ.
- Make absolute data residency statements that ignore metadata/logging realities.
- Email restricted audit artifacts; use the evidence request process under NDA.

---

## Owners
- **Primary owner (publication + comms):** Talia Benson (GTM Program Management)
- **Security technical approver:** Marcus Lin
- **GRC approver:** Olga Petrov
- **Product alignment approver:** Naomi Feldman
- **Legal (as needed):** Michael Grant

For urgent deal deadlines, follow the Security Review Escalation & Handoff playbook (see related pages).

- Olga Petrov
- Naomi Feldman

---

## Review cadence
- **Quarterly**: scheduled review of the FAQ (even if no changes are expected) by Security + GRC + Product Security PM.
- **Out-of-cycle**: allowed when (a) customer feedback indicates ambiguity, (b) product behavior changes, or (c) compliance posture wording must be updated.

---

## How to request a change (internal)
1) File a request in the enablement intake channel or the designated ticketing workflow (link from the enablement kit index).
2) Include:
   - the exact question and customer context,
   - the proposed wording (if any),
   - the deal urgency/date needed,
   - whether the change affects Hosted/Dedicated/Private.
3) Talia Benson coordinates drafting; reviewers sign off per “Required approvals.”
4) Once published, Sales Enablement posts the new version and updates the enablement kit index.

---

## Quick “do / don’t” reminders for Sales and SE
**Do**
- Cite the version and published date in RFP submissions.
- Use approved snippets for high-frequency controls (SSO/RBAC/audit logs).
- Escalate exceptions using the questionnaire exceptions policy.

**Don’t**
- Promise certifications (e.g., ISO 27001) or attestations not explicitly stated in the current FAQ.
- Make absolute data residency statements that ignore metadata/logging realities.
- Email restricted audit artifacts; use the evidence request process under NDA.

---

## Owners
- **Primary owner (publication + comms):** Talia Benson (GTM Program Management)
- **Security technical approver:** Marcus Lin
- **GRC approver:** Olga Petrov
- **Product alignment approver:** Naomi Feldman
- **Legal (as needed):** Michael Grant

For urgent deal deadlines, follow the Security Review Escalation & Handoff playbook (see related pages).

title: Demo Talk Track Script v1 (AE/SE) — Redwood Inference
preview:
## Purpose
This page is the canonical spoken script for the Redwood demo. It is designed to:
- Keep every AE/SE-led demo consistent.
- Make transitions and “why it matters” crisp (not a feature tour).
- Provide optional branches by audience (Hosted API, Dedicated capacity, Private/VPC/security).
- Include safe questions to ask live, and safe claims to make.
- Provide timing guidance for 20/30/45 minute variants.

This script is tied to the deck (see related pages). If a live step becomes brittle, switch to **Backup Mode** (screenshots/recording) and keep the narrative intact.

---

## Roles & handoffs
**AE (driver):** framing, qualification, business value, next steps.

**SE (driver):** live demo steps, architecture explanations, reliability/economics levers, technical Q&A.

**Handoff cues are explicitly marked** below as:
- **[AE]** to speak
- **[SE]** to speak
- **[LIVE]** action in demo tenant / demo app

---

## Pre-demo setup (do not do on the customer call)
**Owner:** SE (or AE if self-running)

1) Confirm demo tenant health:
- Run demo healthcheck and verify: model pins healthy, synthetic traffic running, dashboards populated, quota OK, streaming OK.
- Ensure you have a scoped key for the specific demo project.

2) Choose the route/persona preset:
- “App Engineer / Hosted API” (default)
- “ML Platform / Dedicated controls”
- “Security reviewer / Private + controls”

3) Open tabs in advance (minimize tab switching during the call):
- Demo app (chat + tool calling + structured output)
- Console: Overview dashboard + Cost/Latency breakdown + Traces + Rollouts view
- (Optional) Architecture diagram slide or screenshot for Private/VPC narrative

**Never** paste real customer data. Use the pre-seeded synthetic prompts and datasets only.

---

## Quick discovery (2–4 minutes)
**Goal:** pick the right branch and earn permission to skip irrelevant material.

**[AE]**
“Before we jump in, can I ask two quick questions so we focus on what matters most?”

**[AE] Questions (pick 2–3):**
1) “Is your biggest constraint today latency, cost at scale, or reliability/uptime?”
2) “Are you serving in one environment today—hosted—or do you need dedicated/private options?”
3) “What does ‘production-ready’ mean for you: SLOs, auditability, data isolation, or rollout control?”
4) “What’s the shape of traffic: spiky vs steady? Mostly short prompts or longer sessions?”

**[AE]**
“Perfect. We’ll show (1) the core API experience, (2) how you control production economics—latency vs cost—and (3) how we handle reliability with graceful degradation. If you want, we’ll close with a quick enterprise/security view.”

**[SE]**
“I’ll keep the live part tight and call out where teams typically get surprised in production.”

---

## Standard 30-minute run-of-show (recommended)
- 0:00–0:04 Discovery + framing
- 0:04–0:08 Problem + why now + Redwood thesis (deck)
- 0:08–0:18 Core API live demo (streaming + structured output + tool calling + retries)
- 0:18–0:24 Production economics live demo (batching/caching + token-level cost/latency)
- 0:24–0:27 Reliability live demo (fallback routing + graceful degradation)
- 0:27–0:30 Console tour + next steps

**20-minute exec variant:** do Core API (8 min), economics (5 min), reliability narrative (2 min), 3-min next steps.

**45-minute technical variant:** include Console traces/rollouts deeper and add architecture Q&A (Private/Dedicated).

---

## Script (slide-aligned)

### 1) Opening framing (deck: title + agenda)
**[AE]**
“Today isn’t about picking a model on a slide. Most teams can get a model response. The hard part is serving it in production—predictable latency, controllable cost, and reliability when things go wrong.”

**[AE]**
“Redwood is a developer-first inference platform for running and optimizing LLM inference across hosted, dedicated, and private deployments. Same API and operational tooling across all three.”

**Transition line (use verbatim):**
**[AE]**
“I’m going to hand it to Markus to show the fastest path from ‘hello world’ to a production-shaped integration—with the knobs teams need once usage grows.”


### 2) Core API demo (LIVE) — streaming + structured output + tool calling + retries
**Goal:** show the integration feels simple, but includes production-grade behaviors.

**[SE]**
“I’ll start with the core API experience. I’ll keep the prompt simple, and focus on three things teams care about once they ship: streaming, structured outputs, and tool calling.”

**[LIVE]**
Open the demo app scenario runner: “Customer Support Triage” (or default “Product Q&A with tools”).

**[SE] (streaming setup)**
“First: streaming. The important point is not that tokens stream—it’s that your app can render partial results quickly and keep the user experience responsive under load.”

**[LIVE]**
Run a chat completion with streaming enabled.

**[SE] (callout while tokens stream)**
“While this streams, note we capture token-level latency and cost in the Console, so you can diagnose where time and spend goes—prompt processing, generation, retries, and tool calls.”

**[SE] (structured output transition)**
“Next: structured output. In production, you don’t want a ‘best effort’ paragraph—you want a schema you can trust.”

**[LIVE]**
Run a scenario that returns JSON with a fixed schema (e.g., {"priority":..., "category":..., "next_action":...}).

**[SE]**
“Here the model is constrained to a specific JSON schema. If it violates the schema, the app can handle it deterministically—retry, fallback, or show a safe error.”

**[SE] (tool calling transition)**
“Now tool calling. We’ll let the model decide when to call a tool, and we’ll show how that’s tracked and debuggable.”

**[LIVE]**
Run a tool-calling demo (e.g., ‘lookup_order_status(order_id)’ or ‘search_knowledge_base(query)’). Show tool call arguments and the tool result.

**[SE]**
“Two things to notice: (1) the tool call arguments are visible, and (2) we can trace the full request path—so when something fails, you can tell whether it was the model, the tool, or a downstream dependency.”

**[SE] (retries / robustness)**
“And finally: retries. In production you need predictable behavior when a region is noisy or capacity is constrained. We’ll show how policies handle that—without the application doing heroics.”

**[LIVE]**
Trigger a controlled transient failure (or show the pre-canned trace) and demonstrate retry/fallback policy in action.

**[Optional audience question (ask once)]**
**[SE]**
“Quick check—do you care more about absolute lowest latency, or more about predictable tail latency at p95/p99?”


### 3) Production economics (LIVE) — cost vs latency, batching/caching toggles, token breakdown
**Goal:** make economics concrete and controllable; avoid vague “we’re cheaper.”

**Transition line (use verbatim):**
**[AE]**
“Most teams we talk to start with quality, then quickly hit a second problem: unit economics. Let’s show how Redwood makes that a set of measurable levers.”

**[SE]**
“Production economics is the combination of throughput, latency, and cost per output token—under your real traffic shape. Redwood’s philosophy is: you should be able to *see* and *control* the tradeoffs.”

**[LIVE]**
Open Console → Cost & Latency breakdown for the demo project (pre-seeded traffic should populate).

**[SE] (callouts)**
“Here’s a token-level breakdown. You can see:
- prompt tokens vs output tokens
- time spent in queueing vs compute
- cache hit rate
- batching effectiveness
This is what lets teams move from guessing to engineering.”

**[LIVE]**
Flip an economics preset (e.g., “Latency-optimized” → “Cost-optimized”) for the demo route.

**[SE]**
“I’m changing the serving policy. Under the hood, this adjusts batching and caching behavior and selects an execution profile tuned for the request distribution.”

**[SE] (exact phrasing to prevent over-claiming)**
“We’re not claiming magic. We’re making the knobs explicit, and tying them to metrics so you can choose what’s right for your product.”

**[LIVE]**
Re-run the same prompt(s) and show: latency change, cost change, and a higher cache hit rate (if applicable).

**[AE] (ROI anchoring, keep it brief)**
“If you’re doing meaningful volume, small per-request savings compound quickly. The key is you don’t have to rebuild your stack to get those wins.”

**Branch prompt:**
**[AE]**
“Is your traffic more ‘bursty user-facing’ or more ‘steady background processing’?”

**[SE] (if bursty)**
“Great—then tail latency and autoscaling behavior matter most. We’ll talk about capacity and fallback next.”

**[SE] (if steady)**
“Great—then batching efficiency and cache reuse typically drive the biggest savings, and you can push cost down while keeping predictable latency.”


### 4) Reliability & graceful degradation (LIVE) — fallback routing by compatible model/region
**Goal:** show a real plan for failures; emphasize “stays online.”

**Transition line (use verbatim):**
**[SE]**
“Now the part that only shows up once you’re in production: what happens when capacity is constrained, a model version changes, or a region has a bad day.”

**[SE]**
“In Redwood you can define routing policies with safe fallbacks—compatible model variants and regions—so your application degrades gracefully instead of going hard down.”

**[LIVE]**
Open Console → Routing / Policy / Rollouts (depending on UI). Show the pinned demo route and allowed fallback policy.

**[SE] (callout)**
“Two important ideas:
1) You can **pin** model versions for stability.
2) You can define **allowed fallbacks** for resilience—so the system has a pre-approved plan instead of improvising.”

**[LIVE]**
Trigger the controlled failure mode (capacity constrained / region disabled) and show fallback activation (trace + route decision).

**[SE] (exact phrasing)**
“The promise isn’t ‘nothing ever fails.’ The promise is: when something fails, you can keep serving with a known behavior, and you can see exactly what happened.”

**[AE] (tie back to business impact)**
“For customer-facing experiences, this is the difference between a brief quality degradation and a full outage.”


### 5) Observability & operations (Console tour) — dashboards, traces, errors, rollouts
**Goal:** show the operational surface area; avoid clicking through flaky pages.

**[SE]**
“I’ll end with the Console. This is what replaces a bunch of bespoke glue: usage analytics, tracing, error analysis, and rollout controls.”

**[LIVE]**
Console → Overview dashboard (show traffic, latency percentiles, error rate).

**[SE]**
“We keep this demo environment seeded with synthetic traffic so you can see realistic distributions—p50, p95, p99—rather than empty graphs.”

**[LIVE]**
Open a single trace and point out: request metadata, model selection, tool call, retry/fallback, token breakdown.

**[SE]**
“This is how teams debug: one place to understand what the system did and why.”

**Optional (only for technical audiences): rollouts**
**[SE]**
“If you’re running evals or changing versions, rollouts matter. You can canary, A/B, and define automatic fallback if metrics regress.”


### 6) Enterprise/security & deployment modes (mostly narrative; screenshots acceptable)
Use this section when a security reviewer is present, or when procurement is likely.

**Transition line:**
**[AE]**
“Before we wrap, do you want a quick view of how this looks for enterprise security and deployment options?”

#### Branch A: Hosted API (default)
**[AE]**
“For teams moving fast, hosted gets you to value quickly—simple API, strong observability, and clear economics.”

#### Branch B: Dedicated capacity
**[AE]**
“For predictable throughput and isolation, Dedicated gives you reserved capacity with policies and SLAs tailored to production usage.”

**[SE] (noisy neighbor objection phrasing)**
“The point is removing uncertainty. With dedicated capacity you’re not competing with other tenants for the same pool, and you can plan throughput in advance.”

#### Branch C: Private / VPC / on-prem
**[AE]**
“For regulated workloads, Private deployments provide a private control plane with customer-managed networking, plus key management integrations and audit logs.”

**[SE] (safe details)**
“Typical controls we highlight are: SSO/SAML, RBAC, audit logs, retention controls, and customer-managed networking. We can go deep with your security team with the right artifacts after the call.”

**Do NOT improvise on:** certifications, data processing terms, or specific residency guarantees. Use the Security FAQ page and route to security/compliance if asked.


### 7) Close & next steps (2–4 minutes)
**[AE]**
“Let me summarize what we saw:
1) A production-shaped API: streaming, structured outputs, tool calling.
2) Explicit economics levers with measurable impact.
3) Reliability with pinned versions and graceful fallback routing.
4) Operational tooling in the Console to debug and roll out safely.”

**[AE] (ask for permission + next step)**
“Based on what you’re trying to build, the next step is usually either:
- a technical deep dive on routing/economics, or
- a short POC plan with success metrics.
Which would be more useful?”

**[AE] (POC success criteria prompts, pick 1–2)**
- “What latency target do we need to hit at p95 under expected load?”
- “What’s your monthly volume and cost sensitivity?”
- “Do we need SSO/audit logs before production?”

**[SE]**
“If we do a POC, we’ll propose a tight evaluation plan—prompt set, expected outputs, and regression alerts—so we can prove performance and quality, not just demo it.”

---

## Objection-handling modules (drop-in scripts)
Use these verbatim to avoid over-claiming.

### Objection: “We can just call an API provider / cloud endpoint.”
**[AE]**
“You absolutely can for early experiments. The difference shows up when you care about predictable unit economics and reliability. Redwood is optimized for production serving: measurable cost/latency levers, operational tooling, and deployment flexibility when requirements change.”

### Objection: “Is this just an inference engine?”
**[SE]**
“The runtime performance matters, but the product is the platform around it: routing policies, caching/batching controls, rollouts, tracing, and a consistent API across hosted/dedicated/private.”

### Objection: “What happens when a model changes or capacity tightens?”
**[SE]**
“We pin versions for stability and define allowed fallbacks for resilience. That gives you a controlled path instead of unexpected behavior at runtime.”

### Objection: “Do you support enterprise security controls?”
**[AE]**
“Yes—SSO/SAML, RBAC, audit logs, retention controls, and options for private networking. We can share the specific security FAQ and schedule a dedicated security review if helpful.”

### Objection: “Your pricing seems confusing (usage vs commits).”
**[AE]**
“Hosted is usage-based—great for variable demand. Dedicated is a capacity commitment—great for predictable throughput and isolation. We’ll map the right model based on your traffic shape and SLOs.”

---

## Audience-based emphasis (quick guide)
**App engineer:** start with Core API; keep Dedicated/Private light unless asked.

**ML platform lead:** emphasize economics, routing policies, rollouts, and throughput predictability.

**Security reviewer:** keep live steps minimal; move to controls, auditability, and deployment architecture with screenshots.

**Procurement/executive sponsor:** 20-minute variant; focus on outcomes, risk reduction, and next steps.

---

## Timing guidance & pacing notes (field-learned)
- If the customer is highly technical, do not rush the traces: one good trace is worth five dashboards.
- If the customer is exec-heavy, do not spend time on UI navigation: show one live response + one economics lever + one reliability story.
- If anything fails live, say: “This is why we invest in demo reliability—let me switch to a backup view so we stay on schedule.” Then go to screenshots/recording.

---

## Backup Mode (when live demo is brittle)
Use this if:
- dashboards are empty
- rate limits/quota hits
- streaming disconnects
- model availability issue

**[SE] backup line (use verbatim):**
“Rather than burn your time debugging a demo environment, I’m going to switch to our backup view. It shows the same flows—streaming, tool calling, and routing decisions—with the traces captured.”

Then:
- Show annotated screenshots of Console dashboards/traces/rollouts.
- Use the same narrative beats as the live flow.
- End with next steps as normal.

---

## Safe-sharing rules (internal reminders)
- Use only synthetic data and the seeded demo prompts.
- Do not share internal URLs, tenant identifiers, or healthcheck outputs on-screen.
- If asked for SOC2/DPA/security evidence: share only via approved channels and links from the Security FAQ page.
- Do not promise specific SLAs, residency guarantees, or roadmap items on the call. Route to the AE owner + legal/security where needed.

---

## Change log
- 2026-02-12: Refined reliability module wording (“graceful degradation”); added exact backup lines; clarified Dedicated vs Hosted phrasing.
- 2025-12-03: Added EMEA/data residency emphasis branch and security reviewer guidance.
- 2025-11-14: Initial v1 published for pilot AEs/SEs.

**[SE]**
“If you’re running evals or changing versions, rollouts matter. You can canary, A/B, and define automatic fallback if metrics regress.”


### 6) Enterprise/security & deployment modes (mostly narrative; screenshots acceptable)
Use this section when a security reviewer is present, or when procurement is likely.

**Transition line:**
**[AE]**
“Before we wrap, do you want a quick view of how this looks for enterprise security and deployment options?”

#### Branch A: Hosted API (default)
**[AE]**
“For teams moving fast, hosted gets you to value quickly—simple API, strong observability, and clear economics.”

#### Branch B: Dedicated capacity
**[AE]**
“For predictable throughput and isolation, Dedicated gives you reserved capacity with policies and SLAs tailored to production usage.”

**[SE] (noisy neighbor objection phrasing)**
“The point is removing uncertainty. With dedicated capacity you’re not competing with other tenants for the same pool, and you can plan throughput in advance.”

#### Branch C: Private / VPC / on-prem
**[AE]**
“For regulated workloads, Private deployments provide a private control plane with customer-managed networking, plus key management integrations and audit logs.”

**[SE] (safe details)**
“Typical controls we highlight are: SSO/SAML, RBAC, audit logs, retention controls, and customer-managed networking. We can go deep with your security team with the right artifacts after the call.”

**Do NOT improvise on:** certifications, data processing terms, or specific residency guarantees. Use the Security FAQ page and route to security/compliance if asked.


### 7) Close & next steps (2–4 minutes)
**[AE]**
“Let me summarize what we saw:
1) A production-shaped API: streaming, structured outputs, tool calling.
2) Explicit economics levers with measurable impact.
3) Reliability with pinned versions and graceful fallback routing.
4) Operational tooling in the Console to debug and roll out safely.”

**[AE] (ask for permission + next step)**
“Based on what you’re trying to build, the next step is usually either:
- a technical deep dive on routing/economics, or
- a short POC plan with success metrics.
Which would be more useful?”

**[AE] (POC success criteria prompts, pick 1–2)**
- “What latency target do we need to hit at p95 under expected load?”
- “What’s your monthly volume and cost sensitivity?”
- “Do we need SSO/audit logs before production?”

**[SE]**
“If we do a POC, we’ll propose a tight evaluation plan—prompt set, expected outputs, and regression alerts—so we can prove performance and quality, not just demo it.”

---

## Objection-handling modules (drop-in scripts)
Use these verbatim to avoid over-claiming.

### Objection: “We can just call an API provider / cloud endpoint.”
**[AE]**
“You absolutely can for early experiments. The difference shows up when you care about predictable unit economics and reliability. Redwood is optimized for production serving: measurable cost/latency levers, operational tooling, and deployment flexibility when requirements change.”

### Objection: “Is this just an inference engine?”
**[SE]**
“The runtime performance matters, but the product is the platform around it: routing policies, caching/batching controls, rollouts, tracing, and a consistent API across hosted/dedicated/private.”

### Objection: “What happens when a model changes or capacity tightens?”
**[SE]**
“We pin versions for stability and define allowed fallbacks for resilience. That gives you a controlled path instead of unexpected behavior at runtime.”

### Objection: “Do you support enterprise security controls?”
**[AE]**
“Yes—SSO/SAML, RBAC, audit logs, retention controls, and options for private networking. We can share the specific security FAQ and schedule a dedicated security review if helpful.”

### Objection: “Your pricing seems confusing (usage vs commits).”
**[AE]**
“Hosted is usage-based—great for variable demand. Dedicated is a capacity commitment—great for predictable throughput and isolation. We’ll map the right model based on your traffic shape and SLOs.”

---

## Audience-based emphasis (quick guide)
**App engineer:** start with Core API; keep Dedicated/Private light unless asked.

**ML platform lead:** emphasize economics, routing policies, rollouts, and throughput predictability.

**Security reviewer:** keep live steps minimal; move to controls, auditability, and deployment architecture with screenshots.

**Procurement/executive sponsor:** 20-minute variant; focus on outcomes, risk reduction, and next steps.

---

## Timing guidance & pacing notes (field-learned)
- If the customer is highly technical, do not rush the traces: one good trace is worth five dashboards.
- If the customer is exec-heavy, do not spend time on UI navigation: show one live response + one economics lever + one reliability story.
- If anything fails live, say: “This is why we invest in demo reliability—let me switch to a backup view so we stay on schedule.” Then go to screenshots/recording.

---

## Backup Mode (when live demo is brittle)
Use this if:
- dashboards are empty
- rate limits/quota hits
- streaming disconnects
- model availability issue

**[SE] backup line (use verbatim):**
“Rather than burn your time debugging a demo environment, I’m going to switch to our backup view. It shows the same flows—streaming, tool calling, and routing decisions—with the traces captured.”

Then:
- Show annotated screenshots of Console dashboards/traces/rollouts.
- Use the same narrative beats as the live flow.
- End with next steps as normal.

---

## Safe-sharing rules (internal reminders)
- Use only synthetic data and the seeded demo prompts.
- Do not share internal URLs, tenant identifiers, or healthcheck outputs on-screen.
- If asked for SOC2/DPA/security evidence: share only via approved channels and links from the Security FAQ page.
- Do not promise specific SLAs, residency guarantees, or roadmap items on the call. Route to the AE owner + legal/security where needed.

---

## Change log
- 2026-02-12: Refined reliability module wording (“graceful degradation”); added exact backup lines; clarified Dedicated vs Hosted phrasing.
- 2025-12-03: Added EMEA/data residency emphasis branch and security reviewer guidance.
- 2025-11-14: Initial v1 published for pilot AEs/SEs.

title: Demo personas and mapped known-good scenarios (Demo Tenant v1)
preview:
## Purpose
This page defines Redwood’s **standard demo personas** and the **mapped, known-good demo scenarios** each persona cares about. It is used to:
- Keep AE/SE-run demos consistent (same story beats, same proof points)
- Choose the correct “happy path” and “objection path” in real time
- Reduce brittleness by reusing a frozen scenario set (model pins, seeded data, stable dashboards)

**Scope:** Live demo + narrative/screenshot modules for enterprise topics. All data shown must be **synthetic**.

---

## Demo personas (who’s watching and what they need)
We typically have 1–2 primary viewers plus a few observers. Use the discovery questions below to identify the primary persona and run the corresponding path.

### Persona A: ML Platform Lead (or Platform/Infra Engineering)
**What they optimize for:** standardization, SLOs, operational control, multi-team governance, predictable economics.

**Signals you’re talking to them:** asks about routing, canaries, rollbacks, quotas, regions, fleet utilization, or “how do we run this for 10 teams?”

**Primary value themes:**
- Unified inference across hosted/dedicated/private with one operational model
- Reliability + graceful degradation (fallbacks by policy)
- Cost controls that are measurable (batching/caching/quantization profiles) and observable

**Key discovery questions (pick 2–3):**
- “Do you have latency SLOs per feature or per product line?”
- “What’s currently driving cost spikes—long contexts, retries, concurrency, or model drift?”
- “Who owns rollouts and regressions today—app teams, ML team, or a platform group?”


### Persona B: Application Engineer (Product/Backend/Full-stack)
**What they optimize for:** fast integration, predictable behavior, simple APIs/SDKs, minimal ops overhead.

**Signals you’re talking to them:** asks for SDK examples, streaming UX, tool calling, structured outputs, error handling, time-to-first-token.

**Primary value themes:**
- Developer-first API with streaming + structured output + tool calling
- Good defaults, reproducible behavior, transparent latency/cost
- “Works in production” story: retries, timeouts, fallbacks

**Key discovery questions (pick 2–3):**
- “Is your UX streaming-first? What’s the acceptable time-to-first-token?”
- “Are you building agentic workflows (tool calling) or mostly single-shot generation?”
- “Where do you see failures today—429s, timeouts, or weird output formatting?”


### Persona C: Security Reviewer (Security Engineering / GRC / IT)
**What they optimize for:** access control, auditability, data handling, residency, retention, vendor risk.

**Signals you’re talking to them:** asks about SSO/SAML, RBAC, audit logs, encryption, retention, VPC/private, SOC2.

**Primary value themes:**
- Clear deployment boundaries: Hosted vs Dedicated vs Private
- Least-privilege + audit logs + retention controls
- Data minimization: synthetic demo, customer controls in production

**Key discovery questions (pick 2–3):**
- “Do you require SSO/SAML on day one for any vendor?”
- “Any residency constraints (EU-only, US-only) or specific cloud requirements?”
- “What’s your log retention policy and audit expectations (who did what/when)?”


### Persona D: Procurement / Finance / Legal (or VP-level sponsor)
**What they optimize for:** predictable spend, contracting clarity, risk management, SLA alignment.

**Signals you’re talking to them:** asks about pricing units, commits, SLAs, DPA, termination terms, support tiers, budget.

**Primary value themes:**
- Clear ROI framing: $/request and $/token improvements via batching/caching and better utilization
- Dedicated capacity for predictability and isolation
- SLA readiness and escalation paths

**Key discovery questions (pick 2–3):**
- “Is your preference usage-based to start, or do you need predictable monthly spend?”
- “Do you have a required uptime/latency SLA for customer-facing features?”
- “What’s the procurement path: security review first, or commercial alignment first?”

---

## Known-good demo scenarios (frozen set)
These scenarios are the canonical demo building blocks. They are designed to work end-to-end in the demo tenant with pinned model versions and seeded telemetry.

**Legend:**
- **Live** = we run it in the demo tenant during the call
- **Narrative/Screens** = talk track + screenshots (used as backup mode and for security/procurement modules)

### Scenario catalog (IDs)
S1. Core API (Chat + Streaming + Structured Output + Tool Calling) — **Live**
S2. Production economics (Batching/Caching toggles + token-level breakdown) — **Live**
S3. Reliability (Fallback routing across model variants/regions + graceful degradation) — **Live**
S4. Observability (Console dashboards + traces + error analysis + rollout view) — **Live**
S5. Enterprise/Security (SSO/SAML, RBAC, audit logs, retention controls) — **Narrative/Screens**
S6. Deployment modes (Hosted vs Dedicated vs Private/VPC/on-prem architecture) — **Narrative/Screens**
S7. Commercial packaging (usage-based + Dedicated commits + POC credits guardrails) — **Narrative**

**Important guardrails:**
- Demo data is synthetic; do not import customer prompts, logs, or datasets.
- Do not “improvise” settings changes outside the runbook; keep to scenario steps to avoid brittle screens.
- If anything looks off, switch to **Backup Mode**: screenshots/recording + verbal explanation.

---

## Persona → scenario mapping (recommended path)
Use this as the default flow. Adjust timing by audience and meeting length.

### Mapping table
Persona: ML Platform Lead
- Must show: **S2, S3, S4**
- Optional: **S6** (if private/dedicated comes up)
- Avoid deep dives on: tool calling minutiae unless asked

Persona: Application Engineer
- Must show: **S1, S4**
- Optional: **S2** (if cost/latency is discussed)
- Avoid deep dives on: procurement/security unless they ask

Persona: Security Reviewer
- Must show: **S5, S6** (screens/narrative)
- Optional: **S4** (auditability in Console if safe; otherwise screenshots)
- Avoid: showing raw internal logs/tenant admin panels beyond approved screenshots

Persona: Procurement/Finance/Legal
- Must show: **S7** (narrative), plus 1 short proof scenario (**S2** preferred)
- Optional: **S6** (Dedicated predictability / Private options)
- Avoid: low-level runtime details (kernels, KV cache internals)

---

## Scenario details (what to demonstrate and what “good” looks like)
The sections below define the purpose, proof points, and success criteria for each scenario.

### S1 — Core API demo (streaming + structured output + tool calling)
**Audience fit:** Application Engineer (primary); also useful as a quick “wow” opener for execs.

**What you prove:**
- Streaming is smooth and stable (time-to-first-token feels fast)
- Structured output reduces integration friction
- Tool calling works with clear retries/error handling

**Live steps (high level):**
1) Start a chat completion with **streaming on**.
2) Switch to a **structured output** response format (JSON schema / constrained output) and show parseable results.
3) Trigger a **tool call** (e.g., lookup/compute) and show the tool result feeding back into the model response.
4) Briefly highlight retries/timeouts behavior (“production-ready defaults”).

**Success criteria:**
- Streaming response begins quickly (no awkward silence)
- Structured response validates (no trailing commentary / malformed JSON)
- Tool call completes without manual intervention

**Common failure modes + backup:**
- Streaming disconnect → switch to non-streaming, narrate streaming benefits, show screenshot/recording.
- Output formatting drift → switch to canned known-good prompt preset and re-run.


### S2 — Production economics (batching/caching + token-level breakdown)
**Audience fit:** ML Platform Lead; also valuable for Procurement when anchored to ROI.

**What you prove:**
- Redwood provides **clear levers** to trade off latency vs cost
- Cost is observable at a **token-level** and attributable by route/model
- Improvements are measurable within minutes using seeded + synthetic traffic

**Live steps (high level):**
1) Show baseline: route metrics (latency percentiles + cost per 1k tokens / request-level breakdown).
2) Enable/adjust batching and caching (as per demo preset) and re-run the same workload.
3) Compare before/after: highlight the cost reduction and what changed (queueing vs GPU efficiency).

**Success criteria:**
- Console shows clear “before vs after” deltas
- You can explain tradeoffs in plain language (“slightly higher p50 for much lower $/token at peak load”)

**Common failure modes + backup:**
- Dashboards empty → run synthetic traffic generator (or switch to pre-captured screenshots).
- Confusing metrics view → use the demo preset dashboard link and a fixed time range.


### S3 — Reliability (fallback routing + graceful degradation)
**Audience fit:** ML Platform Lead; also good objection-handling when “what happens when capacity is tight?” comes up.

**What you prove:**
- Routing policies keep apps online during capacity/model changes
- Fallback is controlled and explainable (compatible model family/region)
- Customers can pin versions and rollout safely

**Live steps (high level):**
1) Show a route pinned to a model/version (demo config).
2) Simulate constrained capacity or an intentionally failing upstream (demo toggle / safe fault injection).
3) Show fallback activation and the resulting service continuity.
4) Tie back to operational control: canary/A-B and rollback story.

**Success criteria:**
- Fallback occurs in a deterministic, explainable way
- The end user experience stays acceptable (no hard outage)

**Common failure modes + backup:**
- Fallback doesn’t trigger → use screenshots of routing policy + pre-captured run output.


### S4 — Observability (Console dashboards + traces + error analysis + rollouts)
**Audience fit:** Everyone; tailor depth.

**What you prove:**
- You can answer: “What’s slow, what’s expensive, what’s failing?” quickly
- Rollouts are operationally safe (canary/A-B) and visible
- Errors are triageable (not just “something broke”) with actionable breakdowns

**Live steps (high level):**
1) Open dashboards for the seeded demo project: latency, cost, error rate.
2) Drill into a request trace: show per-stage timing and token breakdown.
3) Show error analysis view (synthetic error scenario) and how you’d triage.
4) Show rollout view (canary/A-B) and how you’d revert.

**Success criteria:**
- The dashboard looks “alive” (non-empty, realistic distributions)
- You can drill down without dead links or confusing empty states

**Common failure modes + backup:**
- Empty dashboards → synthetic traffic job; if still empty, use annotated screenshots.


### S5 — Enterprise/security module (SSO/SAML, RBAC, audit logs, retention)
**Audience fit:** Security Reviewer; sometimes Procurement/Legal.

**What you prove:**
- Redwood supports enterprise controls without requiring bespoke work
- Access is least-privilege and auditable
- Data retention/residency have a clear story by deployment mode

**Format:** Narrative + approved screenshots only (unless explicitly cleared for live views).

**Topics to cover (pick based on questions):**
- SSO/SAML integration approach and enforcement
- RBAC roles (org/project/environment scopes)
- Audit logs: admin actions, key changes, access events
- Retention controls and log storage policies
- Encryption and key management integrations (KMS/HSM options)

**Success criteria:**
- Security reviewer leaves with clear next steps (questionnaire, SOC2 letter request path, architecture review)


### S6 — Deployment modes module (Hosted vs Dedicated vs Private)
**Audience fit:** Security + ML Platform Lead; helpful for execs.

**What you prove:**
- Redwood can meet requirements across isolation and compliance needs
- Same API/operational model across environments

**Format:** Narrative + architecture slide (and optional diagrams).

**Key talking points:**
- Hosted: fastest to start; standard controls
- Dedicated: predictable throughput + data isolation; SLA-backed targets
- Private: VPC/on-prem options for regulated workloads; customer-managed networking + key management integrations


### S7 — Commercial module (pricing, commits, POC credits)
**Audience fit:** Procurement/Finance; exec sponsor.

**What you prove:**
- Spend can be predictable and controllable
- Redwood supports both usage-based and committed capacity motions

**Key talking points:**
- Usage-based for Hosted API (simple entry)
- Dedicated commits for predictable throughput and unit economics at scale
- Guardrails for pilots/POCs (credits approval process; what we do/don’t promise)

---

## “Known-good” scenario checklist (what must be true for a live run)
Before a customer-facing demo, verify:
- Demo tenant healthcheck is green (quota, model availability, dashboard ingestion)
- Model pins are intact for demo routes
- Synthetic traffic has populated dashboards in the last ~15 minutes
- Scoped API keys are valid and not near expiry/rotation boundary
- Backup assets are available (screenshots/recording) in case of live failure

(Execution details live in the SE runbook; this page defines the intended scenarios.)

---

## Talk-track guidance: how to choose the path quickly
If you only have 20 minutes:
- App Engineer: S1 → S4 (short) → close
- ML Platform Lead: S2 → S3 (short) → S4 (short) → close
- Security/Procurement-heavy: S6 → S5 → S7 → close

If you have 30 minutes (standard):
- Start with persona-aligned “wow” (S1 or S2)
- Prove production-readiness (S3)
- Land the operational story (S4)
- End with deployment + next steps (S6 + agreed follow-up)

---

## Ownership and change control
This mapping is intentionally stable. Changes must be coordinated because they affect:
- Scripted talk track and deck flow
- Seeded data and synthetic traffic patterns
- Model pinning and fallback policies

**Owners:**
- Messaging/personas: Soojin Lee (PMM), Camila Reyes (Marketing)
- Live scenario integrity: Markus Klein (SE), Simran Kaur (Enterprise demo)
- Reliability/runbook alignment: Sean Gallagher (SRE)

**Change process:** create a short PRD update + dry run before publishing scenario changes to the field.

2) Drill into a request trace: show per-stage timing and token breakdown.
3) Show error analysis view (synthetic error scenario) and how you’d triage.
4) Show rollout view (canary/A-B) and how you’d revert.

**Success criteria:**
- The dashboard looks “alive” (non-empty, realistic distributions)
- You can drill down without dead links or confusing empty states

**Common failure modes + backup:**
- Empty dashboards → synthetic traffic job; if still empty, use annotated screenshots.


### S5 — Enterprise/security module (SSO/SAML, RBAC, audit logs, retention)
**Audience fit:** Security Reviewer; sometimes Procurement/Legal.

**What you prove:**
- Redwood supports enterprise controls without requiring bespoke work
- Access is least-privilege and auditable
- Data retention/residency have a clear story by deployment mode

**Format:** Narrative + approved screenshots only (unless explicitly cleared for live views).

**Topics to cover (pick based on questions):**
- SSO/SAML integration approach and enforcement
- RBAC roles (org/project/environment scopes)
- Audit logs: admin actions, key changes, access events
- Retention controls and log storage policies
- Encryption and key management integrations (KMS/HSM options)

**Success criteria:**
- Security reviewer leaves with clear next steps (questionnaire, SOC2 letter request path, architecture review)


### S6 — Deployment modes module (Hosted vs Dedicated vs Private)
**Audience fit:** Security + ML Platform Lead; helpful for execs.

**What you prove:**
- Redwood can meet requirements across isolation and compliance needs
- Same API/operational model across environments

**Format:** Narrative + architecture slide (and optional diagrams).

**Key talking points:**
- Hosted: fastest to start; standard controls
- Dedicated: predictable throughput + data isolation; SLA-backed targets
- Private: VPC/on-prem options for regulated workloads; customer-managed networking + key management integrations


### S7 — Commercial module (pricing, commits, POC credits)
**Audience fit:** Procurement/Finance; exec sponsor.

**What you prove:**
- Spend can be predictable and controllable
- Redwood supports both usage-based and committed capacity motions

**Key talking points:**
- Usage-based for Hosted API (simple entry)
- Dedicated commits for predictable throughput and unit economics at scale
- Guardrails for pilots/POCs (credits approval process; what we do/don’t promise)

---

## “Known-good” scenario checklist (what must be true for a live run)
Before a customer-facing demo, verify:
- Demo tenant healthcheck is green (quota, model availability, dashboard ingestion)
- Model pins are intact for demo routes
- Synthetic traffic has populated dashboards in the last ~15 minutes
- Scoped API keys are valid and not near expiry/rotation boundary
- Backup assets are available (screenshots/recording) in case of live failure

(Execution details live in the SE runbook; this page defines the intended scenarios.)

---

## Talk-track guidance: how to choose the path quickly
If you only have 20 minutes:
- App Engineer: S1 → S4 (short) → close
- ML Platform Lead: S2 → S3 (short) → S4 (short) → close
- Security/Procurement-heavy: S6 → S5 → S7 → close

If you have 30 minutes (standard):
- Start with persona-aligned “wow” (S1 or S2)
- Prove production-readiness (S3)
- Land the operational story (S4)
- End with deployment + next steps (S6 + agreed follow-up)

---

## Ownership and change control
This mapping is intentionally stable. Changes must be coordinated because they affect:
- Scripted talk track and deck flow
- Seeded data and synthetic traffic patterns
- Model pinning and fallback policies

**Owners:**
- Messaging/personas: Soojin Lee (PMM), Camila Reyes (Marketing)
- Live scenario integrity: Markus Klein (SE), Simran Kaur (Enterprise demo)
- Reliability/runbook alignment: Sean Gallagher (SRE)

**Change process:** create a short PRD update + dry run before publishing scenario changes to the field.

title: Inference Platform Sales Brief — Developer & Enterprise Messaging
preview:
Purpose
Provide a single, sharable sales brief that the field can use for pre-sales conversations with engineering and platform stakeholders. The document bundles messaging, key competitive differentials, sample pricing guidance, a short customer case example, a pilot offer, objection-handling, and a concise security FAQ for prospects.

1) One-line positioning
Redwood Inference: a developer-first inference platform that delivers predictable latency, measurable unit economics, and a single operational surface across hosted, dedicated, and private deployments.

2) Core value pillars (talking points)
- Performance certainty: predictable p50/p95/p99 tail latency profiles and autoscaling that keeps production SLAs intact.
- Cost controls: batching, KV/prefix caching, and quantization profiles tuned for workload tradeoffs to reduce per-token costs.
- Deployment flexibility: same API/ops experience across Hosted API, Dedicated capacity, and Private (VPC/on‑prem) for regulated workloads.
- Reliability & fallbacks: automatic model-variant fallbacks, regional routing, and transparent degraded-mode behaviors.
- Observability: token-level cost and latency breakdowns, per-route dashboards, and prompt-regression alerts.

3) Elevator pitch (30–60s)
"If your team is building LLM features you care about predictable inference economics and disruption-free rollouts. Redwood gives you a single API and console to ship fast on hosted infrastructure, move to reserved Dedicated capacity for predictable throughput, or run privately behind your network controls — all while lowering per‑token cost through batching, caching, and quantization recommendations, and giving you SLOs and observability you can rely on."

4) Quick technical highlights (for engineering stakeholders)
- Serving runtime: GPU-optimized continuous batching, KV cache-aware execution and quantization-friendly kernels.
- Smart routing: policies for latency, cost, region and customer tier; automated fallback to compatible model variants.
- Observability: per-request token timeline, cost attribution (model, compute, I/O), and alerting for quality regressions tied to labeled prompt sets.
- Integrations: Kubernetes-based Dedicated runtime or OCI-compliant on‑prem package; KMS/HSM integrations for keys; SSO and fine-grained RBAC.

5) Competitive brief — concise battlecard
Competitors considered: OpenAI (hosted), Anthropic, AWS Bedrock, Self-hosted runtimes (KServe/Replicate), Hybrid vendors.
- Differentiators (what to emphasize):
  - Unified product surface across Hosted / Dedicated / Private (most competitors split these experiences).
  - Production-first telemetry: token-level cost + latency timelines and regression alerts (rare at this granularity).
  - Cost control levers exposed to customers: batching, caching, quantization recommendations and policy-based routing.
  - Enterprise-grade private deployment with optional air-gap and KMS/HSM integration.
- Weaknesses / concessions to acknowledge proactively:
  - We do not have the broadest model catalog compared to general model marketplaces; we focus on verified models and performance profiles.
  - For very small teams wanting a purely managed black-box API, OpenAI may be simpler — but lacks deterministic cost controls and private deployment options at scale.

6) Short battlecard table (talk-to bullets)
Product area | Redwood priority message | Competitive contrast
Hosted latency & SLOs | Predictable p95/p99 tail SLOs and circuit-breaker fallbacks | OpenAI: low friction but less transparent unit economics
Cost & TCO | Active batching/caching and quantization recommendations reduce token cost by up to ~30–60% depending on workload | Bedrock: managed but limited cost optimization controls
Private deployment | Single API + VPC/on‑prem option + KMS/HSM | Self-hosted: control but no unified SaaS dev DX; cloud vendors: private options are fragmented
Observability | Token-level tracing and regression alerts tied to prompt sets | Competitors: per-request logs but not consistent token-level cost attribution

7) Pricing guidance (rules of thumb for reps)
- Hosted API: usage-based pricing. Position conservatively: $0.05–0.40 per 1k input+output tokens depending on model class and size. (Use exact SKU pricing from pricing sheet for quotes.)
- Embeddings: price per 1k vectors; sample guidance: $0.10–0.50 per 1k depending on dimensionality and throughput SLA.
- Dedicated (reserved capacity): monthly committed spend with sliding discounts; typical deals for medium enterprise: $15k–$150k/month depending on GPU class and throughput.
- Private deployment: licensing + support (one-time platform fee + annual support). Typical structure: platform license tier (based on vCPU/GPU capacity) + support SLA and optional deployment engineering.
- Commerce levers for negotiation: minimum commitment length (6/12/24 months), committed throughput (reserve QPS), feature packs (longer retention, advanced observability), professional services (onboarding, private deployment).
- Sample quick TCO framing (for buyer’s infra team):
  - Self-hosting 8x A100 equivalent cluster (incl. infra, infra ops, 24/7 rotations): N months to ROI; approximate monthly infra+ops = $80k–$150k+; Redwood Dedicated can often be more cost-effective when factoring ops and autoscaling.

8) Pilot / proof-of-value offer (standard prescriptive play)
- Typical pilot: 8–12 weeks; goals: validate latency and throughput, measure per-token cost reductions (batching + quant), and trial rollout.
- Standard pilot package: $25k–$50k in hosted credits + engineering concierge + 2 dedicated onboarding sessions. For larger enterprise pilots, propose a time-boxed Dedicated proof with discounted first 3 months.
- Success criteria (agreed at kickoff): p99 latency <= target, per-token cost reduction >= X% vs baseline, integration of telemetry into buyer’s dashboards.

9) Short customer case example (sales-friendly)
Customer: Acme Retail (mid-market, e-commerce personalization)
Problem: Sudden latency spikes and unpredictable inference costs after model upgrade.
Solution: Migrated recommendation inference to Redwood Dedicated, enabled KV prefix caching for user sessions, and applied a 4-bit quantization profile for the candidate model.
Outcome:
- 42% reduction in per‑query cost vs self-hosted baseline (measured over 90 days).
- 3x reduction in p99 tail latency during holiday peak thanks to autoscaling + prefetched KV cache.
- Successful rollback plan + canary rollouts avoided a customer-facing defect during a model update.

10) Objection handling (common reps scripts)
- "We can self-host cheaper": Ask about total cost of ops, SRE headcount, and the unpredictability of traffic patterns. Offer a TCO side-by-side that includes on-call, queues, and engineering time.
- "We don’t want vendor lock-in": Emphasize API parity across Hosted/Dedicated/Private and that customers can pin model versions and export logs/model snapshots. Private deployment option removes the lock-in concern.
- "Latency matters more than cost": Lead with SLO guarantees, smart routing by region and model variant, and examples of p99 improvements from caching and routing policies.
- "Security/compliance concerns": Point to SOC2, KMS integration, VPC option, and our security FAQ below; offer to involve Security/Early Access for technical deep dives.

11) Security FAQ for prospects (short answers reps can use)
Q: Where does customer data live?
A: Hosted: customer data is stored in region-specific clusters with configurable retention. Dedicated: data is isolated within your dedicated capacity. Private: data never leaves your VPC/on-prem environment unless explicitly configured.
Q: How is data encrypted?
A: In transit via TLS 1.2+/TLS 1.3, at rest with AES-256. Key material can be customer-managed via KMS/HSM integrations.
Q: Do you support on‑prem/air‑gapped installs?
A: Yes—Redwood Private supports VPC deployments and an on‑prem package with an air‑gap option for regulated environments (deployment engineering required).
Q: What compliance certifications do you maintain?
A: SOC 2 Type II in-scope for the hosted control plane; evidence packages available under NDA. We provide artifacts for ISO/Sec assessments during enterprise sales cycles.
Q: Who has access to logs or model outputs?
A: Access is controlled via SSO and RBAC. Audit logs are retained per the contract and can be exported. For the highest assurance customers we enable restricted log access and give them the option to ship logs to their SIEM.
Q: What about incident response and notification?
A: We maintain an incident response playbook, dedicated SE escalation paths for customers with SLAs, and monthly security reviews for large accounts. Full post-incident reports available for incidents affecting customer data.
Q: Can we use our own keys?
A: Yes. KMS/HSM integration supported for Hosted and Dedicated. Private deployments can be configured to use an entirely customer-managed key hierarchy.

12) Sales collateral & assets to attach / point to
- Link to one‑pager product summary (internal): /confluence/product-overview/redwood-one-pager
- Technical deep-dive deck for infra teams: /confluence/eng-serving-runtime/runtime-architecture-deck
- Cost-savings calculator (spreadsheet): shared drive link (ask revenue ops to attach)
- Security evidence pack (NDA required): contact security@redwood-inference.com

13) Call to action / next steps for a rep
- PRE-SALE: run quick discovery focused on SLA targets (latency, throughput), sensitive data flags (PII/regulatory), and internal ops capacity.
- PROPOSAL: choose Hosted pilot vs Dedicated proof depending on target scale & compliance. Include onboarding and PS time in the commercial proposal.
- TECHNICAL DEPLOYMENT: schedule 2 technical workshops: (1) integration + observability, (2) SLAs + rollout plan.

Appendix: sample SLA terms (summary)
- Availability: 99.95% monthly for Dedicated and Hosted control plane (detailed exclusions in legal SLA).
- Latency target: baseline p95 token generation target provided per model in performance profiles; can be contractually scoped for Dedicated capacity.
- Support: standard SLAs (8x5) available; enterprise SLAs (24x7) available as add-ons.

Notes / versioning
- Maintainer: Sofia Patel (GTM).
- Last review: 2026-02-10 by Marcus Lin (Head of Product GTM).
- Use this document as the canonical field kit for Q1–Q2 2026. Update pricing SKUs and compliance artifacts links quarterly.
